The physics-inspired approach provides new insights into sequence modeling and practical benefits for long-context processing.

title: ImplicitMemBench: Measuring Unconscious Behavioral Adaptation in Large Language Models
summary: Existing memory benchmarks for LLM agents evaluate explicit recall of facts, yet overlook implicit memory where experience becomes automated behavior without conscious retrieval. This gap is critical: effective assistants must automatically apply learned procedures or avoid failed actions without explicit reminders. We introduce ImplicitMemBench, the first systematic benchmark evaluating implicit memory through three cognitively grounded constructs drawn from standard cognitive-science accounts of non-declarative memory: Procedural Memory (one-shot skill acquisition after interference), Priming (theme-driven bias via paired experimental/control instances), and Classical Conditioning (Conditioned Stimulus--Unconditioned Stimulus (CS--US) associations shaping first decisions). Our 300-item suite employs a unified Learning/Priming-Interfere-Test protocol with first-attempt scoring. Evaluation of 17 models reveals severe limitations: no model exceeds 66% overall, with top performers DeepSeek-R1 (65.3%), Qwen3-32B (64.1%), and GPT-5 (63.0%) far below human baselines. Analysis uncovers dramatic asymmetries (inhibition 17.6% vs. preference 75.0%) and universal bottlenecks requiring architectural innovations beyond parameter scaling. ImplicitMemBench reframes evaluation from "what agents recall" to "what they automatically enact".

AI.tldr: ImplicitMemBench is the first benchmark evaluating implicit memory in LLM agents through three cognitive constructs: procedural memory, priming, and classical conditioning, revealing severe limitations in current models.
AI.motivation: Existing memory benchmarks only evaluate explicit recall of facts but overlook implicit memory where experience becomes automated behavior without conscious retrieval. This gap is critical because effective AI assistants must automatically apply learned procedures or avoid failed actions without explicit reminders.
AI.method: Introduces ImplicitMemBench, a systematic benchmark evaluating implicit memory through three cognitively grounded constructs: 1) Procedural Memory (one-shot skill acquisition after interference), 2) Priming (theme-driven bias via paired experimental/control instances), and 3) Classical Conditioning (CS-US associations shaping first decisions). Uses a 300-item suite with unified Learning/Priming-Interfere-Test protocol and first-attempt scoring.
AI.result: Evaluation of 17 models reveals severe limitations: no model exceeds 66% overall performance. Top performers are DeepSeek-R1 (65.3%), Qwen3-32B (64.1%), and GPT-5 (63.0%), all far below human baselines. Analysis uncovers dramatic asymmetries (inhibition 17.6% vs. preference 75.0%) and universal bottlenecks requiring architectural innovations beyond parameter scaling.
AI.conclusion: ImplicitMemBench reframes evaluation from "what agents recall" to "what they automatically enact," highlighting significant gaps in current LLM agents' implicit memory capabilities and pointing to the need for architectural innovations rather than just parameter scaling.

title: Towards Rapid Constitutive Model Discovery from Multi-Modal Data: Physics Augmented Finite Element Model Updating (paFEMU)
summary: Recent progress in AI-enabled constitutive modeling has concentrated on moving from a purely data-driven paradigm to the enforcement of physical constraints and mechanistic principles, a concept referred to as physics augmentation. Classical phenomenological approaches rely on selecting a pre-defined model and calibrating its parameters, while machine learning methods often focus on discovery of the model itself. Sparse regression approaches lie in between, where large libraries of pre-defined models are probed during calibration. Sparsification in the aforementioned paradigm, but also in the context of neural network architecture, has been shown to enable interpretability, uncertainty quantification, but also heterogeneous software integration due to the low-dimensional nature of the resulting models. Most works in AI-enabled constitutive modeling have also focused on data from a single source, but in reality, materials modeling workflows can contain data from many different sources (multi-modal data), and also from testing other materials within the same materials class (multi-fidelity data). In this work, we introduce physics augmented finite element model updating (paFEMU), as a transfer learning approach that combines AI-enabled constitutive modeling, sparsification for interpretable model discovery, and finite element-based adjoint optimization utilizing multi-modal data. This is achieved by combining simple mechanical testing data, potentially from a distinct material, with digital image correlation-type full-field data acquisition to ultimately enable rapid constitutive modeling discovery. The simplicity of the sparse representation enables easy integration of neural constitutive models in existing finite element workflows, and also enables low-dimensional updating during transfer learning.

AI.tldr: A physics-augmented finite element model updating (paFEMU) approach combines AI-enabled constitutive modeling, sparse representation for interpretability, and adjoint optimization to discover constitutive models from multi-modal/multi-fidelity data, enabling easy integration with existing FEM workflows.
AI.motivation: Current AI-enabled constitutive modeling needs better integration of physical constraints and mechanistic principles (physics augmentation). Most approaches focus on single-source data, but real-world materials modeling involves multi-modal data (different sources) and multi-fidelity data (different materials within same class).
AI.method: Proposes paFEMU as a transfer learning approach combining: 1) AI-enabled constitutive modeling, 2) Sparsification for interpretable model discovery, 3) Finite element-based adjoint optimization using multi-modal data. Combines simple mechanical testing data with digital image correlation full-field data for rapid constitutive model discovery.
AI.result: The sparse representation enables easy integration of neural constitutive models in existing finite element workflows and allows low-dimensional updating during transfer learning, addressing limitations of purely data-driven approaches.
AI.conclusion: paFEMU provides a framework for interpretable, physics-augmented constitutive modeling that can handle real-world multi-modal/multi-fidelity data while maintaining compatibility with existing finite element workflows through sparse representations.

title: Revise: A Framework for Revising OCRed text in Practical Information Systems with Data Contamination Strategy
summary: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have significantly improved the field of Document AI, demonstrating remarkable performance on document understanding tasks such as question answering. However, existing approaches primarily focus on solving specific tasks, lacking the capability to structurally organize and manage document information. To address this limitation, we propose Revise, a framework that systematically corrects errors introduced by OCR at the character, word, and structural levels. Specifically, Revise employs a comprehensive hierarchical taxonomy of common OCR errors and a synthetic data generation strategy that realistically simulates such errors to train an effective correction model. Experimental results demonstrate that Revise effectively corrects OCR outputs, enabling more structured representation and systematic management of document contents. Consequently, our method significantly enhances downstream performance in document retrieval and question answering tasks, highlighting the potential to overcome the structural management limitations of existing Document AI frameworks.

AI.tldr: Revise is a framework that corrects OCR errors at multiple levels to enable better structural organization of document information, improving downstream document AI tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based Document AI approaches focus on specific tasks but lack capabilities for structural organization and management of document information. OCR errors compound this problem by introducing inaccuracies that prevent proper structural representation.
AI.method: Revise systematically corrects OCR errors at character, word, and structural levels using a comprehensive hierarchical taxonomy of common OCR errors and a synthetic data generation strategy that realistically simulates such errors to train an effective correction model.
AI.result: Experimental results show Revise effectively corrects OCR outputs, enabling more structured representation and systematic management of document contents. This significantly enhances downstream performance in document retrieval and question answering tasks.
AI.conclusion: Revise overcomes structural management limitations of existing Document AI frameworks by addressing OCR errors comprehensively, demonstrating the potential for better-organized document information systems.

title: Structured Distillation of Web Agent Capabilities Enables Generalization
summary: Frontier LLMs can navigate complex websites, but their cost and reliance on third-party APIs make local deployment impractical. We introduce Agent-as-Annotators, a framework that structures synthetic trajectory generation for web agents by analogy to human annotation roles, replacing the Task Designer, Annotator, and Supervisor with modular LLM components. Using Gemini 3 Pro as teacher, we generate 3,000 trajectories across six web environments and fine-tune a 9B-parameter student with pure supervised learning on the 2,322 that pass quality filtering. The resulting model achieves 41.5% on WebArena, surpassing closed-source models such as Claude 3.5 Sonnet (36.0%) and GPT-4o (31.5%) under the same evaluation protocol, and nearly doubling the previous best open-weight result (Go-Browse, 21.7%). Capabilities transfer to unseen environments, with an 18.2 percentage point gain on WorkArena L1 (an enterprise platform never seen during training) and consistent improvements across three additional benchmarks. Ablations confirm that each pipeline component contributes meaningfully, with Judge filtering, evaluation hints, and reasoning traces each accounting for measurable gains. These results demonstrate that structured trajectory synthesis from a single frontier teacher is sufficient to produce competitive, locally deployable web agents. Project page: https://agent-as-annotators.github.io

AI.tldr: Agent-as-Annotators framework uses frontier LLMs to generate synthetic training trajectories for web agents, enabling efficient fine-tuning of smaller models that outperform larger closed-source counterparts on web navigation tasks.
AI.motivation: Frontier LLMs are effective at web navigation but too expensive for local deployment due to cost and reliance on third-party APIs. There's a need for competitive, locally deployable web agents that don't require massive human annotation efforts.
AI.method: Introduces Agent-as-Annotators framework that structures synthetic trajectory generation by analogy to human annotation roles: Task Designer, Annotator, and Supervisor replaced with modular LLM components. Uses Gemini 3 Pro as teacher to generate 3,000 trajectories across 6 web environments, then fine-tunes a 9B-parameter student model with supervised learning on 2,322 quality-filtered trajectories.
AI.result: The resulting model achieves 41.5% on WebArena, surpassing Claude 3.5 Sonnet (36.0%) and GPT-4o (31.5%), and nearly doubling previous best open-weight result (Go-Browse, 21.7%). Shows strong transfer to unseen environments with 18.2 percentage point gain on WorkArena L1 and consistent improvements across three additional benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Structured trajectory synthesis from a single frontier teacher is sufficient to produce competitive, locally deployable web agents without extensive human annotation, with each pipeline component contributing meaningfully to performance gains.

title: Beyond Stochastic Exploration: What Makes Training Data Valuable for Agentic Search
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become an effective approach for advancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) through the strategic integration of external search engines. However, current RL-based search agents often rely on a process of stochastic exploration guided by carefully crafted outcome rewards, leading to inefficient reasoning trajectories and unstable training. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework, Hierarchical Experience (HiExp), to enhance the performance and training stability of search agents. Specifically, we extract empirical knowledge through contrastive analysis and a multi-level clustering mechanism, transforming raw reasoning trajectories into hierarchical experience knowledge. By leveraging experience-aligned training, we effectively regularize stochastic exploration, evolving it into a strategic and experience-driven search process. Extensive evaluations on multiple complex agentic search and mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that our approach not only achieves substantial performance gains but also exhibits strong cross-task and cross-algorithm generalization.

AI.tldr: HiExp: A hierarchical experience framework that transforms raw reasoning trajectories into knowledge to guide reinforcement learning search agents, improving performance and training stability.
AI.motivation: Current RL-based search agents for LLMs use inefficient stochastic exploration guided by outcome rewards, leading to inefficient reasoning trajectories and unstable training.
AI.method: Extract empirical knowledge through contrastive analysis and multi-level clustering, transforming raw reasoning trajectories into hierarchical experience knowledge, then use experience-aligned training to regularize stochastic exploration.
AI.result: Achieves substantial performance gains on multiple complex agentic search and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, with strong cross-task and cross-algorithm generalization.
AI.conclusion: The HiExp framework successfully enhances search agent performance and training stability by transforming stochastic exploration into strategic, experience-driven search processes.

title: PolicyLong: Towards On-Policy Context Extension
comment: Work in progress. Correspondence to ucaswu@tencent.com or wuxing@iie.ac.cn
summary: Extending LLM context windows is hindered by scarce high-quality long-context data. Recent methods synthesize data with genuine long-range dependencies via information-theoretic verification, selecting contexts that reduce a base model's predictive entropy. However, their single-pass offline construction with a fixed model creates a fundamental off-policy gap: the static screening landscape misaligns with the model's evolving capabilities, causing the training distribution to drift. We propose PolicyLong, shifting data construction towards a dynamic on-policy paradigm. By iteratively re-executing data screening (entropy computation, retrieval, and verification) using the current model, PolicyLong ensures the training distribution tracks evolving capabilities, yielding an emergent self-curriculum. Crucially, both positive and hard negative contexts derive from the current model's entropy landscape, co-evolving what the model learns to exploit and resist. Experiments on RULER, HELMET, and LongBench-v2 (Qwen2.5-3B) show PolicyLong consistently outperforms EntropyLong and NExtLong, with gains growing at longer contexts (e.g., +2.54 at 128K on RULER), confirming the value of on-policy data evolution.

AI.tldr: PolicyLong addresses the off-policy gap in long-context data synthesis by introducing dynamic on-policy screening that evolves with model capabilities, outperforming static methods especially at longer contexts.
AI.motivation: Extending LLM context windows is limited by scarce high-quality long-context data. Existing methods use static, offline data construction with fixed models, creating an off-policy gap where the training distribution doesn't align with the model's evolving capabilities, causing distribution drift.
AI.method: PolicyLong introduces a dynamic on-policy paradigm that iteratively re-executes data screening (entropy computation, retrieval, and verification) using the current model. This creates a self-curriculum where both positive and hard negative contexts derive from the model's evolving entropy landscape, ensuring training distribution tracks model capabilities.
AI.result: Experiments on RULER, HELMET, and LongBench-v2 with Qwen2.5-3B show PolicyLong consistently outperforms EntropyLong and NExtLong, with gains increasing at longer contexts (e.g., +2.54 at 128K on RULER), confirming the value of on-policy data evolution.
AI.conclusion: On-policy data construction that dynamically evolves with model capabilities is crucial for effective long-context training, outperforming static methods and showing increasing benefits at longer context lengths.

title: Activation Steering for Aligned Open-ended Generation without Sacrificing Coherence
summary: Alignment in LLMs is more brittle than commonly assumed: misalignment can be triggered by adversarial prompts, benign fine-tuning, emergent misalignment, and goal misgeneralization. Recent evidence suggests that some misalignment behaviors are encoded as linear structure in activation space, making it tractable via steering, while safety alignment has been shown to govern the first few output tokens primarily, leaving subsequent generation unguarded. These findings motivate activation steering as a lightweight runtime defense that continuously corrects misaligned activations throughout generation. We evaluate three methods: Steer-With-Fixed-Coeff (SwFC), which applies uniform additive steering, and two novel projection-aware methods, Steer-to-Target-Projection (StTP) and Steer-to-Mirror-Projection (StMP), that use a logistic regression decision boundary to selectively intervene only on tokens whose activations fall below distributional thresholds. Using malicious system prompts as a controlled proxy for misalignment, we evaluate under two threat models (dishonesty and dismissiveness) and two architectures (Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, Qwen3-32B). All methods substantially recover target traits (honesty and compassion) while preserving coherence. StTP and StMP better maintain general capabilities (MMLU, MT-Bench, AlpacaEval) and produce less repetition in multi-turn conversations.

AI.tldr: Activation steering as lightweight runtime defense against LLM misalignment, with novel projection-aware methods outperforming uniform steering in preserving capabilities.
AI.motivation: LLM alignment is brittle and can be triggered by various factors; recent evidence shows misalignment behaviors are encoded as linear structure in activation space, making them tractable via steering, while safety alignment primarily governs only first few tokens, leaving subsequent generation unguarded.
AI.method: Three activation steering methods: Steer-With-Fixed-Coeff (SwFC) applies uniform additive steering; Steer-to-Target-Projection (StTP) and Steer-to-Mirror-Projection (StMP) use logistic regression decision boundary to selectively intervene only on tokens whose activations fall below distributional thresholds.
AI.result: All methods substantially recover target traits (honesty and compassion) while preserving coherence; StTP and StMP better maintain general capabilities (MMLU, MT-Bench, AlpacaEval) and produce less repetition in multi-turn conversations compared to uniform steering.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering serves as effective lightweight runtime defense against LLM misalignment, with projection-aware methods offering better capability preservation than uniform steering approaches.

title: Information-Theoretic Requirements for Gradient-Based Task Affinity Estimation in Multi-Task Learning
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures. Accepted at workshop on AI for Accelerated Materials Design, Foundation Models for Science: Real-World Impact and Science-First Design, and Generative and Experimental Perspectives for Biomolecular Design at ICLR 2026
summary: Multi-task learning shows strikingly inconsistent results -- sometimes joint training helps substantially, sometimes it actively harms performance -- yet the field lacks a principled framework for predicting these outcomes. We identify a fundamental but unstated assumption underlying gradient-based task analysis: tasks must share training instances for gradient conflicts to reveal genuine relationships. When tasks are measured on the same inputs, gradient alignment reflects shared mechanistic structure; when measured on disjoint inputs, any apparent signal conflates task relationships with distributional shift. We discover this sample overlap requirement exhibits a sharp phase transition: below 30% overlap, gradient-task correlations are statistically indistinguishable from noise; above 40%, they reliably recover known biological structure. Comprehensive validation across multiple datasets achieves strong correlations and recovers biological pathway organization. Standard benchmarks systematically violate this requirement -- MoleculeNet operates at <5% overlap, TDC at 8-14% -- far below the threshold where gradient analysis becomes meaningful. This provides the first principled explanation for seven years of inconsistent MTL results.

AI.tldr: Gradient-based task analysis requires substantial sample overlap (30-40%) to reveal genuine task relationships; most benchmarks operate far below this threshold, explaining inconsistent multi-task learning results.
AI.motivation: Multi-task learning shows inconsistent results with no principled framework to predict when joint training helps or harms performance. The field lacks understanding of why gradient-based task analysis yields unreliable outcomes.
AI.method: Identified the fundamental assumption that tasks must share training instances for gradient conflicts to reveal genuine relationships. Analyzed sample overlap requirements through empirical validation across multiple datasets, discovering a sharp phase transition around 30-40% overlap for meaningful gradient-task correlations.
AI.result: Discovered sample overlap exhibits a sharp phase transition: below 30% overlap, gradient-task correlations are statistically indistinguishable from noise; above 40%, they reliably recover known biological structure. Validation shows strong correlations and recovery of biological pathway organization. Standard benchmarks systematically violate this requirement (MoleculeNet: <5%, TDC: 8-14% overlap).
AI.conclusion: Provides the first principled explanation for seven years of inconsistent multi-task learning results. Gradient-based task analysis requires substantial sample overlap to be meaningful, and most existing benchmarks operate far below the necessary threshold, making their gradient analysis unreliable.

title: Aligning Agents via Planning: A Benchmark for Trajectory-Level Reward Modeling
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures, accepted to ACL 2026 main conference
summary: In classical Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), Reward Models (RMs) serve as the fundamental signal provider for model alignment. As Large Language Models evolve into agentic systems capable of autonomous tool invocation and complex reasoning, the paradigm of reward modeling faces unprecedented challenges--most notably, the lack of benchmarks specifically designed to assess RM capabilities within tool-integrated environments. To address this gap, we present Plan-RewardBench, a trajectory-level preference benchmark designed to evaluate how well judges distinguish preferred versus distractor agent trajectories in complex tool-using scenarios. Plan-RewardBench covers four representative task families -- (i) Safety Refusal, (ii) Tool-Irrelevance / Unavailability, (iii) Complex Planning, and (iv) Robust Error Recovery -- comprising validated positive trajectories and confusable hard negatives constructed via multi-model natural rollouts, rule-based perturbations, and minimal-edit LLM perturbations. We benchmark representative RMs (generative, discriminative, and LLM-as-Judge) under a unified pairwise protocol, reporting accuracy trends across varying trajectory lengths and task categories. Furthermore, we provide diagnostic analyses of prevalent failure modes. Our results reveal that all three evaluator families face substantial challenges, with performance degrading sharply on long-horizon trajectories, underscoring the necessity for specialized training in agentic, trajectory-level reward modeling. Ultimately, Plan-RewardBench aims to serve as both a practical evaluation suite and a reusable blueprint for constructing agentic planning preference data.

AI.tldr: Plan-RewardBench is a new benchmark designed to evaluate reward models in complex tool-using agent scenarios, revealing significant performance degradation on long-horizon trajectories across all evaluator types.
AI.motivation: As LLMs evolve into agentic systems with autonomous tool use, existing reward modeling approaches face challenges due to lack of benchmarks specifically designed for tool-integrated environments where traditional RLHF methods may fall short.
AI.method: The authors create Plan-RewardBench covering four task families (Safety Refusal, Tool-Irrelevance/Unavailability, Complex Planning, Robust Error Recovery) with validated positive trajectories and confusable hard negatives constructed via multi-model natural rollouts, rule-based perturbations, and minimal-edit LLM perturbations. They benchmark generative, discriminative, and LLM-as-Judge reward models under a unified pairwise protocol.
AI.result: All three evaluator families (generative, discriminative, LLM-as-Judge) face substantial challenges, with performance degrading sharply on long-horizon trajectories. The benchmark reveals prevalent failure modes and the need for specialized training in agentic, trajectory-level reward modeling.
AI.conclusion: Plan-RewardBench serves as both a practical evaluation suite and a reusable blueprint for constructing agentic planning preference data, highlighting the necessity for specialized reward modeling approaches in complex tool-using environments.

title: QaRL: Rollout-Aligned Quantization-Aware RL for Fast and Stable Training under Training--Inference Mismatch
summary: Large language model (LLM) reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines are often bottlenecked by rollout generation, making end-to-end training slow. Recent work mitigates this by running rollouts with quantization to accelerate decoding, which is the most expensive stage of the RL loop. However, these setups destabilize optimization by amplifying the training-inference gap: rollouts are operated at low precision, while learning updates are computed at full precision. To address this challenge, we propose QaRL (Rollout Alignment Quantization-Aware RL), which aligns training-side forward with the quantized rollout to minimize mismatch. We further identify a failure mode in quantized rollouts: long-form responses tend to produce repetitive, garbled tokens (error tokens). To mitigate these problems, we introduce TBPO (Trust-Band Policy Optimization), a sequence-level objective with dual clipping for negative samples, aimed at keeping updates within the trust region. On Qwen3-30B-A3B MoE for math problems, QaRL outperforms quantized-rollout training by +5.5 while improving stability and preserving low-bit throughput benefits.

AI.tldr: QaRL (Quantization-Aware RL) addresses training instability in LLM RL by aligning quantized rollouts with training, and TBPO (Trust-Band Policy Optimization) solves repetitive token errors in long responses, achieving +5.5 improvement on math problems while maintaining low-bit throughput.
AI.motivation: LLM RL pipelines are bottlenecked by slow rollout generation. While quantization accelerates rollout decoding, it creates a training-inference gap where rollouts use low precision but learning uses full precision, causing optimization instability and error accumulation in long-form responses.
AI.method: Proposes QaRL (Rollout Alignment Quantization-Aware RL) to align training-side forward passes with quantized rollouts, minimizing precision mismatch. Also introduces TBPO (Trust-Band Policy Optimization), a sequence-level objective with dual clipping for negative samples that keeps updates within a trust region to address repetitive/garbled token errors in quantized rollouts.
AI.result: On Qwen3-30B-A3B MoE for math problems, QaRL outperforms quantized-rollout training by +5.5 points while improving training stability and preserving the low-bit throughput benefits of quantization.
AI.conclusion: QaRL successfully addresses the training-inference gap in quantized RL pipelines by aligning precision domains and introducing trust region constraints, enabling stable optimization while maintaining the computational benefits of low-bit quantization for faster rollout generation.

title: Grounding Clinical AI Competency in Human Cognition Through the Clinical World Model and Skill-Mix Framework
comment: Code, data (Clinical AI Skill-Mix dimension specifications), and an exploratory dashboard are available at https://github.com/Sdamirsa/Clinical-World-Model
summary:
The competency of any intelligent agent is bounded by its formal account of the world in which it operates. Clinical AI lacks such an account. Existing frameworks address evaluation, regulation, or system design in isolation, without a shared model of the clinical world to connect them. We introduce the Clinical World Model, a framework that formalizes care as a tripartite interaction among Patient, Provider, and Ecosystem. To formalize how any agent, whether human or artificial, transforms information into clinical action, we develop parallel decision-making architectures for providers, patients, and AI agents, grounded in validated principles of clinical cognition.
  The Clinical AI Skill-Mix operationalizes competency through eight dimensions. Five define the clinical competency space (condition, phase, care setting, provider role, and task) and three specify how AI engages human reasoning (assigned authority, agent facing, and anchoring layer). The combinatorial product of these dimensions yields a space of billions of distinct competency coordinates. A central structural implication is that validation within one coordinate provides minimal evidence for performance in another, rendering the competency space irreducible. The framework supplies a common grammar through which clinical AI can be specified, evaluated, and bounded across stakeholders. By making this structure explicit, the Clinical World Model reframes the field's central question from whether AI works to in which competency coordinates reliability has been demonstrated, and for whom.

AI.tldr: The Clinical World Model is a new framework that formalizes clinical care as interactions among Patient, Provider, and Ecosystem, introducing an 8-dimensional Clinical AI Skill-Mix to specify AI competency across billions of distinct clinical scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current clinical AI lacks a formal account of the world in which it operates. Existing frameworks address evaluation, regulation, or system design in isolation without a shared model to connect them, creating a fragmented understanding of AI competency in clinical settings.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Clinical World Model framework that formalizes care as tripartite interactions among Patient, Provider, and Ecosystem. They develop parallel decision-making architectures for providers, patients, and AI agents, and operationalize competency through the 8-dimensional Clinical AI Skill-Mix (5 clinical competency dimensions + 3 AI-human engagement dimensions).
AI.result: The combinatorial product of the 8 dimensions yields a space of billions of distinct competency coordinates, demonstrating that validation in one coordinate provides minimal evidence for performance in another. This makes the competency space irreducible and fundamentally changes how we assess AI reliability in clinical settings.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a common grammar for specifying, evaluating, and bounding clinical AI across stakeholders. It reframes the field's central question from "whether AI works" to "in which competency coordinates reliability has been demonstrated, and for whom," making explicit the structural complexity of clinical AI competency.

title: Bit-by-Bit: Progressive QAT Strategy with Outlier Channel Splitting for Stable Low-Bit LLMs
summary: Training LLMs at ultra-low precision remains a formidable challenge. Direct low-bit QAT often suffers from convergence instability and substantial training costs, exacerbated by quantization noise from heavy-tailed outlier channels and error accumulation across layers. To address these issues, we present Bit-by-Bit, a progressive QAT framework with outlier channel splitting. Our approach integrates three key components: (1) block-wise progressive training that reduces precision stage by stage, ensuring stable initialization for low-bit optimization; (2) nested structure of integer quantization grids to enable a "train once, deploy any precision" paradigm, allowing a single model to support multiple bit-widths without retraining; (3) rounding-aware outlier channel splitting, which mitigates quantization error while acting as an identity transform that preserves the quantized outputs. Furthermore, we follow microscaling groups with E4M3 scales, capturing dynamic activation ranges in alignment with OCP/NVIDIA standards. To address the lack of efficient 2-bit kernels, we developed custom operators for both W2A2 and W2A16 configurations, achieving up to 11$\times$ speedup over BF16. Under W2A2 settings, Bit-by-Bit significantly outperforms baselines like BitDistiller and EfficientQAT on both Llama2/3, achieving a loss of only 2.25 WikiText2 PPL compared to full-precision models.

AI.tldr: Bit-by-Bit is a progressive quantization-aware training framework that enables stable ultra-low precision LLM training through block-wise progressive training, nested integer quantization grids, and rounding-aware outlier channel splitting.
AI.motivation: Training LLMs at ultra-low precision faces challenges with convergence instability, high training costs, quantization noise from outlier channels, and error accumulation across layers. Existing low-bit QAT methods struggle with these issues.
AI.method: Three key components: 1) Block-wise progressive training that reduces precision stage by stage for stable initialization. 2) Nested integer quantization grids enabling "train once, deploy any precision" without retraining. 3) Rounding-aware outlier channel splitting to mitigate quantization error while preserving quantized outputs. Uses microscaling groups with E4M3 scales and custom 2-bit kernels.
AI.result: Achieves 11× speedup over BF16 with custom 2-bit kernels. Under W2A2 settings, significantly outperforms baselines like BitDistiller and EfficientQAT on Llama2/3, achieving only 2.25 WikiText2 PPL loss compared to full-precision models.
AI.conclusion: Bit-by-Bit effectively addresses ultra-low precision LLM training challenges, offering stable convergence, reduced training costs, and superior performance compared to existing methods while supporting flexible deployment at multiple bit-widths.

title: HiRO-Nav: Hybrid ReasOning Enables Efficient Embodied Navigation
summary: Embodied navigation agents built upon large reasoning models (LRMs) can handle complex, multimodal environmental input and perform grounded reasoning per step to improve sequential decision-making for long-horizon tasks. However, a critical question remains: \textit{how can the reasoning capabilities of LRMs be harnessed intelligently and efficiently for long-horizon navigation tasks?} In simple scenes, agents are expected to act reflexively, while in complex ones they should engage in deliberate reasoning before acting.To achieve this, we introduce \textbf{H}ybr\textbf{i}d \textbf{R}eas\textbf{O}ning \textbf{Nav}igation (\textbf{HiRO-Nav}) agent, the first kind of agent capable of adaptively determining whether to perform thinking at every step based on its own action entropy. Specifically, by examining how the agent's action entropy evolves over the navigation trajectories, we observed that only a small fraction of actions exhibit high entropy, and these actions often steer the agent toward novel scenes or critical objects. Furthermore, studying the relationship between action entropy and task completion (i.e., Q-value) reveals that improving high-entropy actions contributes more positively to task success.Hence, we propose a tailored training pipeline comprising hybrid supervised fine-tuning as a cold start, followed by online reinforcement learning with the proposed hybrid reasoning strategy to explicitly activate reasoning only for high-entropy actions, significantly reducing computational overhead while improving decision quality. Extensive experiments on the \textsc{CHORES}-$\mathbb{S}$ ObjectNav benchmark showcases that HiRO-Nav achieves a better trade-off between success rates and token efficiency than both dense-thinking and no-thinking baselines.

AI.tldr: HiRO-Nav introduces a hybrid reasoning navigation agent that adaptively activates reasoning only for high-entropy actions, balancing decision quality and computational efficiency for long-horizon embodied navigation.
AI.motivation: Current LRM-based embodied navigation agents face a key challenge: how to intelligently and efficiently harness reasoning capabilities for long-horizon tasks. Simple scenes require reflexive actions while complex scenes need deliberate reasoning, but current approaches either use reasoning at every step (computationally expensive) or no reasoning (poor decision quality).
AI.method: The HiRO-Nav agent adaptively determines whether to perform reasoning at each step based on action entropy. The approach includes: 1) analyzing action entropy evolution to identify that only high-entropy actions (often leading to novel scenes or critical objects) require reasoning, 2) showing that improving high-entropy actions contributes more to task success, 3) implementing hybrid supervised fine-tuning followed by online reinforcement learning with hybrid reasoning strategy that activates reasoning only for high-entropy actions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on the CHORES-S ObjectNav benchmark show HiRO-Nav achieves a better trade-off between success rates and token efficiency than both dense-thinking (reasoning at every step) and no-thinking baselines. The agent reduces computational overhead while improving decision quality.
AI.conclusion: The HiRO-Nav framework successfully demonstrates that adaptive reasoning based on action entropy provides an efficient way to leverage LRM capabilities for embodied navigation, achieving superior performance-computation balance compared to existing approaches.

title: Kuramoto Oscillatory Phase Encoding: Neuro-inspired Synchronization for Improved Learning Efficiency
summary: Spatiotemporal neural dynamics and oscillatory synchronization are widely implicated in biological information processing and have been hypothesized to support flexible coordination such as feature binding. By contrast, most deep learning architectures represent and propagate information through activation values, neglecting the joint dynamics of rate and phase. In this work, we introduce Kuramoto oscillatory Phase Encoding (KoPE) as an additional, evolving phase state to Vision Transformers, incorporating a neuro-inspired synchronization mechanism to advance learning efficiency. We show that KoPE can improve training, parameter, and data efficiency of vision models through synchronization-enhanced structure learning. Moreover, KoPE benefits tasks requiring structured understanding, including semantic and panoptic segmentation, representation alignment with language, and few-shot abstract visual reasoning (ARC-AGI). Theoretical analysis and empirical verification further suggest that KoPE can accelerate attention concentration for learning efficiency. These results indicate that synchronization can serve as a scalable, neuro-inspired mechanism for advancing state-of-the-art neural network models.

AI.tldr: KoPE adds Kuramoto oscillatory phase encoding to Vision Transformers, using neural synchronization mechanisms to improve learning efficiency and structured understanding.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning architectures use only activation values and neglect joint rate-phase dynamics, while biological systems use spatiotemporal neural dynamics and oscillatory synchronization for information processing and coordination.
AI.method: Introduces Kuramoto oscillatory Phase Encoding (KoPE) as an additional evolving phase state to Vision Transformers, incorporating neuro-inspired synchronization mechanisms for enhanced structure learning.
AI.result: KoPE improves training, parameter, and data efficiency; benefits tasks requiring structured understanding (semantic/panoptic segmentation, language alignment, few-shot abstract reasoning); accelerates attention concentration for learning efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Synchronization serves as a scalable, neuro-inspired mechanism for advancing state-of-the-art neural networks by incorporating biological-like oscillatory dynamics.

title: From Phenomenological Fitting to Endogenous Deduction: A Paradigm Leap via Meta-Principle Physics Architecture
summary:
The essence of current neural network architectures is phenomenological fitting: they learn input-output statistical correlations via massive parameters and data, yet lack intrinsic understanding of the fundamental principles governing physical reality. This paper proposes a paradigm leap from pure phenomenological fitting to the fusion of phenomenological fitting and endogenous deduction. By embedding physical meta-principles into neural network architecture, we construct the Meta-Principle Physics Architecture (MPPA).
  Specifically, MPPA embeds three core meta-principles - Connectivity, Conservation, Periodicity - into its architecture, implemented via three core components: the Gravitator realizes Connectivity via standard causal attention; the Energy Encoder implements Conservation via log-domain energy tracking and delayed compensation; the Periodicity Encoder fulfills Periodicity via FFT-based spectral analysis and delayed modulation. These components collaborate via a learnable independent gating fusion mechanism, forming a complete physical cognition framework of 'local relational connectivity - global conservation constraint - evolutionary periodic law'.
  Experiments show MPPA achieves significant improvements: physical reasoning (from near zero to 0.436, 0.436 vs 0.000), 2.18x mathematical task improvement (0.330 vs 0.151), 52% logical task gain (0.456 vs 0.300), and 3.69% lower validation perplexity (259.45 vs 269.40), with only 11.8% more parameters (242.40M vs 216.91M). Notably, MPPA shows strong generalization on out-of-distribution physical scenarios, proving the robustness and interpretability of this principle-embedded design. This work establishes a new theoretical foundation and technical path for next-generation AI with physical common sense, causal reasoning, and mathematical rigor.

AI.tldr: MPPA embeds physical meta-principles (Connectivity, Conservation, Periodicity) into neural architecture, moving from pure phenomenological fitting to principle-guided learning with improved physical reasoning, mathematical, and logical performance.
AI.motivation: Current neural networks lack intrinsic understanding of fundamental physical principles and rely solely on phenomenological fitting through massive parameters and data. There's a need for AI systems with physical common sense, causal reasoning, and mathematical rigor.
AI.method: Proposes Meta-Principle Physics Architecture (MPPA) embedding three core physical meta-principles: Connectivity (via Gravitator with causal attention), Conservation (via Energy Encoder with log-domain energy tracking), and Periodicity (via Periodicity Encoder with FFT-based spectral analysis). Components collaborate through learnable independent gating fusion mechanism.
AI.result: MPPA achieves significant improvements: physical reasoning from near zero to 0.436, 2.18x mathematical task improvement (0.330 vs 0.151), 52% logical task gain (0.456 vs 0.300), and 3.69% lower validation perplexity with only 11.8% more parameters. Shows strong generalization on out-of-distribution physical scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a new theoretical foundation and technical path for next-generation AI with physical common sense, causal reasoning, and mathematical rigor, demonstrating that principle-embedded designs enable robust, interpretable, and generalizable AI systems.

title: Sinkhorn doubly stochastic attention rank decay analysis
summary: The self-attention mechanism is central to the success of Transformer architectures. However, standard row-stochastic attention has been shown to suffer from significant signal degradation across layers. In particular, it can induce rank collapse, resulting in increasingly uniform token representations, as well as entropy collapse, characterized by highly concentrated attention distributions. Recent work has highlighted the benefits of doubly stochastic attention as a form of entropy regularization, promoting a more balanced attention distribution and leading to improved empirical performance. In this paper, we study rank collapse across network depth and show that doubly stochastic attention matrices normalized with Sinkhorn algorithm preserve rank more effectively than standard Softmax row-stochastic ones. As previously shown for Softmax, skip connections are crucial to mitigate rank collapse. We empirically validate this phenomenon on both sentiment analysis and image classification tasks. Moreover, we derive a theoretical bound for the pure self-attention rank decay when using Sinkhorn normalization and find that rank decays to one doubly exponentially with depth, a phenomenon that has already been shown for Softmax.

AI.tldr: Doubly stochastic attention using Sinkhorn normalization prevents rank collapse better than standard Softmax attention, preserving token representation diversity across Transformer layers.
AI.motivation: Standard row-stochastic attention suffers from signal degradation across layers, causing rank collapse (uniform token representations) and entropy collapse (concentrated attention distributions). Recent work shows doubly stochastic attention helps, but theoretical understanding of its rank preservation properties is needed.
AI.method: Study rank collapse across network depth using doubly stochastic attention matrices normalized with the Sinkhorn algorithm. Compare with standard Softmax row-stochastic attention. Analyze skip connections' role. Derive theoretical bound for pure self-attention rank decay with Sinkhorn normalization.
AI.result: Doubly stochastic attention preserves rank more effectively than standard Softmax attention. Skip connections are crucial for mitigating rank collapse in both cases. Theoretical analysis shows rank decays to one doubly exponentially with depth for Sinkhorn normalization (same as Softmax). Empirical validation on sentiment analysis and image classification tasks confirms the phenomenon.
AI.conclusion: Doubly stochastic attention with Sinkhorn normalization is theoretically and empirically superior for preventing rank collapse in Transformers compared to standard Softmax attention, though both suffer doubly exponential rank decay without skip connections.

title: Neural-Symbolic Knowledge Tracing: Injecting Educational Knowledge into Deep Learning for Responsible Learner Modelling
summary: The growing use of artificial intelligence (AI) in education, particularly large language models (LLMs), has increased interest in intelligent tutoring systems. However, LLMs often show limited adaptivity and struggle to model learners' evolving knowledge over time, highlighting the need for dedicated learner modelling approaches. Although deep knowledge tracing methods achieve strong predictive performance, their opacity and susceptibility to bias can limit alignment with pedagogical principles. To address this, we propose Responsible-DKT, a neural-symbolic deep knowledge tracing approach that integrates symbolic educational knowledge (e.g., mastery and non-mastery rules) into sequential neural models for responsible learner modelling. Experiments on a real-world dataset of students' math interactions show that Responsible-DKT outperforms both a neural-symbolic baseline and a fully data-driven PyTorch DKT model across training settings. The model achieves over 0.80 AUC with only 10% of training data and up to 0.90 AUC, improving performance by up to 13%. It also demonstrates improved temporal reliability, producing lower early- and mid-sequence prediction errors and the lowest prediction inconsistency rates across sequence lengths, indicating that prediction updates remain directionally aligned with observed student responses over time. Furthermore, the neural-symbolic approach offers intrinsic interpretability via a grounded computation graph that exposes the logic behind each prediction, enabling both local and global explanations. It also allows empirical evaluation of pedagogical assumptions, revealing that repeated incorrect responses (non-mastery) strongly influence prediction updates. These results indicate that neural-symbolic approaches enhance both performance and interpretability, mitigate data limitations, and support more responsible, human-centered AI in education.

AI.tldr: Responsible-DKT: A neural-symbolic deep knowledge tracing approach that integrates symbolic educational knowledge (like mastery rules) with neural models to create more interpretable and pedagogically-aligned learner models, outperforming data-driven baselines while providing better temporal reliability and interpretability.
AI.motivation: LLMs in education lack adaptivity and struggle to model evolving learner knowledge over time. While deep knowledge tracing methods perform well, they suffer from opacity, susceptibility to bias, and poor alignment with pedagogical principles. There's a need for more interpretable and responsible learner modelling approaches.
AI.method: Proposed Responsible-DKT, a neural-symbolic approach that integrates symbolic educational knowledge (mastery and non-mastery rules) into sequential neural models for knowledge tracing. This creates a grounded computation graph that exposes prediction logic while maintaining predictive power.
AI.result: Experiments on real-world math interaction data show Responsible-DKT outperforms both neural-symbolic baseline and PyTorch DKT model. Achieves >0.80 AUC with only 10% training data and up to 0.90 AUC (13% improvement). Shows improved temporal reliability with lower prediction errors and inconsistency rates, plus intrinsic interpretability via grounded computation graph.
AI.conclusion: Neural-symbolic approaches enhance both performance and interpretability, mitigate data limitations, and support more responsible, human-centered AI in education by enabling local/global explanations and empirical evaluation of pedagogical assumptions.

title: Robust Length Prediction: A Perspective from Heavy-Tailed Prompt-Conditioned Distributions
summary: Output-length prediction is important for efficient LLM serving, as it directly affects batching, memory reservation, and scheduling. For prompt-only length prediction, most existing methods use a one-shot sampled length as the label, implicitly treating each prompt as if it had one true target length. We show that this is unreliable: even under a fixed model and decoding setup, the same prompt induces a \emph{prompt-conditioned output length distribution}, not a deterministic scalar, and this distribution is consistent with \emph{heavy-tailed} behavior. Motivated by this, we cast length prediction as robust estimation from heavy-tailed prompt-conditioned length distributions. We propose prompt-conditioned length distribution (ProD) methods, which construct training targets from multiple independent generations of the same prompt. Two variants are developed to reuse the served LLM's hidden states: \mbox{ProD-M}, which uses a median-based target for robust point prediction, and ProD-D, which uses a distributional target that preserves prompt-conditioned uncertainty. We provide theoretical justifications by analyzing the estimation error under a surrogate model. Experiments across diverse scenarios show consistent gains in prediction quality.

AI.tldr: Length prediction is unreliable when treated as deterministic; actual output length follows heavy-tailed prompt-conditioned distributions. New ProD methods use multiple generations per prompt for robust prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt-only length prediction methods assume each prompt has one true target length, but actual outputs show heavy-tailed distributions. This unreliability affects LLM serving efficiency for batching, memory, and scheduling.
AI.method: Proposed ProD methods: construct training targets from multiple independent generations per prompt. ProD-M uses median-based targets for robust point prediction; ProD-D uses distributional targets preserving uncertainty. Both reuse served LLM's hidden states.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse scenarios show consistent gains in prediction quality. Theoretical analysis provides justification for estimation error under surrogate model.
AI.conclusion: Treating output length as deterministic scalar is flawed; modeling it as heavy-tailed prompt-conditioned distribution with ProD methods improves prediction quality for efficient LLM serving.

title: ACF: A Collaborative Framework for Agent Covert Communication under Cognitive Asymmetry
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures. Submitted to IEEE Signal Processing Letters (SPL). Source code is available at https://github.com/Dwinovo/ACF-Stego
summary: As generative artificial intelligence evolves, autonomous agent networks present a powerful paradigm for interactive covert communication. However, because agents dynamically update internal memories via environmental interactions, existing methods face a critical structural vulnerability: cognitive asymmetry. Conventional approaches demand strict cognitive symmetry, requiring identical sequence prefixes between the encoder and decoder. In dynamic deployments, inevitable prefix discrepancies destroy synchronization, inducing severe channel degradation. To address this core challenge of cognitive asymmetry, we propose the Asymmetric Collaborative Framework (ACF), which structurally decouples covert communication from semantic reasoning via orthogonal statistical and cognitive layers. By deploying a prefix-independent decoding paradigm governed by a shared steganographic configuration, ACF eliminates the reliance on cognitive symmetry. Evaluations on realistic memory-augmented workflows demonstrate that under severe cognitive asymmetry, symmetric baselines suffer severe channel degradation, whereas ACF uniquely excels across both semantic fidelity and covert communication. It maintains computational indistinguishability, enabling reliable secret extraction with provable error bounds, and providing robust Effective Information Capacity guarantees for modern agent networks.

AI.tldr: ACF is a framework that enables reliable covert communication in autonomous agent networks by addressing cognitive asymmetry through orthogonal statistical and cognitive layers.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous agent networks for covert communication suffer from cognitive asymmetry vulnerability - they require strict cognitive symmetry between encoder and decoder (identical sequence prefixes). In dynamic deployments, inevitable prefix discrepancies destroy synchronization and cause severe channel degradation.
AI.method: The Asymmetric Collaborative Framework (ACF) structurally decouples covert communication from semantic reasoning via orthogonal statistical and cognitive layers. It uses a prefix-independent decoding paradigm governed by a shared steganographic configuration, eliminating reliance on cognitive symmetry.
AI.result: Under severe cognitive asymmetry, symmetric baselines suffer severe channel degradation, while ACF uniquely excels across both semantic fidelity and covert communication. ACF maintains computational indistinguishability, enables reliable secret extraction with provable error bounds, and provides robust Effective Information Capacity guarantees.
AI.conclusion: ACF successfully addresses the core challenge of cognitive asymmetry in autonomous agent networks for covert communication, enabling reliable operation in dynamic deployments where conventional symmetric approaches fail.

title: A Systematic Framework for Tabular Data Disentanglement
summary: Tabular data, widely used in various applications such as industrial control systems, finance, and supply chain, often contains complex interrelationships among its attributes. Data disentanglement seeks to transform such data into latent variables with reduced interdependencies, facilitating more effective and efficient processing. Despite the extensive studies on data disentanglement over image, text, or audio data, tabular data disentanglement may require further investigation due to the more intricate attribute interactions typically found in tabular data. Moreover, due to the highly complex interrelationships, direct translation from other data domains results in suboptimal data disentanglement. Existing tabular data disentanglement methods, such as factor analysis, CT-GAN, and VAE face limitations including scalability issues, mode collapse, and poor extrapolation. In this paper, we propose the use of a framework to provide a systematic view on tabular data disentanglement that modularizes the process into four core components: data extraction, data modeling, model analysis, and latent representation extrapolation. We believe this work provides a deeper understanding of tabular data disentanglement and existing methods, and lays the foundation for potential future research in developing robust, efficient, and scalable data disentanglement techniques. Finally, we demonstrate the framework's applicability through a case study on synthetic tabular data generation, showcasing its potential in the particular downstream task of data synthesis.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a framework for tabular data disentanglement that modularizes the process into four components, addressing limitations of existing methods and demonstrating applicability through synthetic data generation.
AI.motivation: Tabular data contains complex attribute interactions making disentanglement challenging. Existing methods like factor analysis, CT-GAN, and VAE face issues with scalability, mode collapse, and poor extrapolation. Direct translations from other data domains (image/text/audio) are suboptimal for tabular data due to its unique characteristics.
AI.method: Proposes a framework that modularizes tabular data disentanglement into four core components: 1) data extraction, 2) data modeling, 3) model analysis, and 4) latent representation extrapolation. Demonstrates the framework through a case study on synthetic tabular data generation.
AI.result: The paper presents a systematic framework for tabular data disentanglement that provides deeper understanding of existing methods and lays foundation for future research. The case study demonstrates the framework's applicability in synthetic tabular data generation for data synthesis tasks.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a comprehensive approach to tabular data disentanglement, addressing limitations of current methods and offering a foundation for developing more robust, efficient, and scalable disentanglement techniques. The framework shows practical value through synthetic data generation applications.

title: U-CECE: A Universal Multi-Resolution Framework for Conceptual Counterfactual Explanations
summary: As AI models grow more complex, explainability is essential for building trust, yet concept-based counterfactual methods still face a trade-off between expressivity and efficiency. Representing underlying concepts as atomic sets is fast but misses relational context, whereas full graph representations are more faithful but require solving the NP-hard Graph Edit Distance (GED) problem. We propose U-CECE, a unified, model-agnostic multi-resolution framework for conceptual counterfactual explanations that adapts to data regime and compute budget. U-CECE spans three levels of expressivity: atomic concepts for broad explanations, relational sets-of-sets for simple interactions, and structural graphs for full semantic structure. At the structural level, both a precision-oriented transductive mode based on supervised Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and a scalable inductive mode based on unsupervised graph autoencoders (GAEs) are supported. Experiments on the structurally divergent CUB and Visual Genome datasets characterize the efficiency-expressivity trade-off across levels, while human surveys and LVLM-based evaluation show that the retrieved structural counterfactuals are semantically equivalent to, and often preferred over, exact GED-based ground-truth explanations.

AI.tldr: U-CECE is a unified multi-resolution framework for concept-based counterfactual explanations that spans atomic concepts, relational sets, and structural graphs, adapting to compute budgets while addressing the expressivity-efficiency trade-off.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental trade-off in concept-based counterfactual explanations: atomic concept representations are efficient but lack relational context, while full graph representations are faithful but require solving NP-hard Graph Edit Distance problems. Current methods struggle to balance expressivity and efficiency.
AI.method: U-CECE introduces a three-level multi-resolution framework: (1) atomic concepts for broad explanations, (2) relational sets-of-sets for simple interactions, and (3) structural graphs for full semantic structure. At the structural level, it offers both precision-oriented transductive mode using supervised GNNs and scalable inductive mode using unsupervised graph autoencoders.
AI.result: Experiments on CUB and Visual Genome datasets demonstrate the efficiency-expressivity trade-off across levels. Human surveys and LVLM-based evaluation show that U-CECE's retrieved structural counterfactuals are semantically equivalent to, and often preferred over, exact GED-based ground-truth explanations.
AI.conclusion: U-CECE provides a unified, model-agnostic framework that adapts to data regimes and compute budgets, successfully balancing the expressivity-efficiency trade-off in concept-based counterfactual explanations across multiple resolution levels.

title: Fraud Detection System for Banking Transactions
summary: The expansion of digital payment systems has heightened both the scale and intricacy of online financial transactions, thereby increasing vulnerability to fraudulent activities. Detecting fraud effectively is complicated by the changing nature of attack strategies and the significant disparity between genuine and fraudulent transactions. This research introduces a machine learning-based fraud detection framework utilizing the PaySim synthetic financial transaction dataset. Following the CRISP-DM methodology, the study includes hypothesis-driven exploratory analysis, feature refinement, and a comparative assessment of baseline models such as Logistic Regression and tree-based classifiers like Random Forest, XGBoost, and Decision Tree. To tackle class imbalance, SMOTE is employed, and model performance is enhanced through hyperparameter tuning with GridSearchCV. The proposed framework provides a robust and scalable solution to enhance fraud prevention capabilities in FinTech transaction systems. Keywords: fraud detection, imbalanced data, HPO, SMOTE

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a machine learning framework for fraud detection in financial transactions using the PaySim dataset, addressing class imbalance with SMOTE and optimizing models with hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: The expansion of digital payment systems has increased online financial transaction volume and complexity, making systems more vulnerable to fraud. Fraud detection is complicated by evolving attack strategies and the significant class imbalance between legitimate and fraudulent transactions.
AI.method: The study follows CRISP-DM methodology with hypothesis-driven exploratory analysis and feature engineering. Uses baseline models including Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Decision Tree. Addresses class imbalance using SMOTE and performs hyperparameter optimization with GridSearchCV.
AI.result: The proposed framework shows robust performance for fraud detection. Comparative assessment of models reveals which approaches work best for imbalanced financial transaction data after SMOTE balancing and hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: The machine learning framework provides a robust and scalable solution to enhance fraud prevention capabilities in FinTech transaction systems, effectively addressing the challenges of evolving fraud patterns and class imbalance.

title: ProMedical: Hierarchical Fine-Grained Criteria Modeling for Medical LLM Alignment via Explicit Injection
summary: Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with high-stakes medical standards remains a significant challenge, primarily due to the dissonance between coarse-grained preference signals and the complex, multi-dimensional nature of clinical protocols. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProMedical, a unified alignment framework grounded in fine-grained clinical criteria. We first construct ProMedical-Preference-50k, a dataset generated via a human-in-the-loop pipeline that augments medical instructions with rigorous, physician-derived rubrics. Leveraging this corpus, we propose the Explicit Criteria Injection paradigm to train a multi-dimensional reward model. Unlike traditional scalar reward models, our approach explicitly disentangles safety constraints from general proficiency, enabling precise guidance during reinforcement learning. To rigorously validate this framework, we establish ProMedical-Bench, a held-out evaluation suite anchored by double-blind expert adjudication. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that optimizing the Qwen3-8B base model via ProMedical-RM-guided GRPO yields substantial gains, improving overall accuracy by 22.3% and safety compliance by 21.7%, effectively rivaling proprietary frontier models. Furthermore, the aligned policy generalizes robustly to external benchmarks, demonstrating performance comparable to state-of-the-art models on UltraMedical. We publicly release our datasets, reward models, and benchmarks to facilitate reproducible research in safety-aware medical alignment.

AI.tldr: ProMedical introduces a clinical alignment framework using fine-grained criteria to bridge the gap between coarse preference signals and complex medical standards, achieving significant performance improvements in medical LLMs.
AI.motivation: There's a dissonance between coarse-grained preference signals used in standard LLM alignment and the complex, multi-dimensional nature of clinical protocols and standards in high-stakes medical applications.
AI.method: 1) Constructed ProMedical-Preference-50k dataset using human-in-the-loop pipeline with physician-derived rubrics; 2) Developed Explicit Criteria Injection paradigm for multi-dimensional reward modeling that disentangles safety constraints from general proficiency; 3) Used GRPO reinforcement learning guided by the reward model.
AI.result: Optimizing Qwen3-8B with ProMedical-RM-guided GRPO improved overall accuracy by 22.3% and safety compliance by 21.7%, rivaling proprietary frontier models. The aligned policy also generalized well to external benchmarks like UltraMedical with state-of-the-art comparable performance.
AI.conclusion: ProMedical demonstrates that fine-grained clinical criteria-based alignment significantly improves medical LLM performance and safety, offering a promising approach for safety-aware medical alignment that bridges the gap between general AI capabilities and specialized clinical requirements.

title: Pruning Extensions and Efficiency Trade-Offs for Sustainable Time Series Classification
summary: Time series classification (TSC) enables important use cases, however lacks a unified understanding of performance trade-offs across models, datasets, and hardware. While resource awareness has grown in the field, TSC methods have not yet been rigorously evaluated for energy efficiency. This paper introduces a holistic evaluation framework that explicitly explores the balance of predictive performance and resource consumption in TSC. To boost efficiency, we apply a theoretically bounded pruning strategy to leading hybrid classifiers - Hydra and Quant - and present Hydrant, a novel, prunable combination of both. With over 4000 experimental configurations across 20 MONSTER datasets, 13 methods, and three compute setups, we systematically analyze how model design, hyperparameters, and hardware choices affect practical TSC performance. Our results showcase that pruning can significantly reduce energy consumption by up to 80% while maintaining competitive predictive quality, usually costing the model less than 5% of accuracy. The proposed methodology, experimental results, and accompanying software advance TSC toward sustainable and reproducible practice.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive evaluation framework for time series classification that balances predictive performance with resource efficiency, featuring novel pruning techniques for hybrid classifiers that can reduce energy consumption by up to 80% with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Time series classification lacks unified understanding of performance trade-offs across models, datasets, and hardware. Current methods haven't been rigorously evaluated for energy efficiency, creating a need for holistic evaluation that considers both predictive performance and resource consumption.
AI.method: Introduced a holistic evaluation framework for TSC that explores balance between predictive performance and resource consumption. Applied theoretically bounded pruning strategy to leading hybrid classifiers (Hydra and Quant). Developed Hydrant, a novel prunable combination of both classifiers. Conducted extensive experiments with over 4000 configurations across 20 MONSTER datasets, 13 methods, and three compute setups.
AI.result: Pruning can significantly reduce energy consumption by up to 80% while maintaining competitive predictive quality, typically costing less than 5% accuracy. Systematic analysis shows how model design, hyperparameters, and hardware choices affect practical TSC performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methodology, experimental results, and accompanying software advance time series classification toward more sustainable and reproducible practice by providing a framework to balance efficiency with predictive performance.

title: Human-AI Collaboration Reconfigures Group Regulation from Socially Shared to Hybrid Co-Regulation
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at AIED 2026. Camera-ready version with updated references
summary: Generative AI (GenAI) is increasingly used in collaborative learning, yet its effects on how groups regulate collaboration remain unclear. Effective collaboration depends not only on what groups discuss, but on how they jointly manage goals, participation, strategy use, monitoring, and repair through co-regulation and socially shared regulation. We compared collaborative regulation between Human-AI and Human-Human groups in a parallel-group randomised experiment with 71 university students completing the same collaborative tasks with GenAI either available or unavailable. Focusing on human discourse, we used statistical analyses to examine differences in the distribution of collaborative regulation across regulatory modes, regulatory processes, and participatory focuses. Results showed that GenAI availability shifted regulation away from predominantly socially shared forms towards more hybrid co-regulatory forms, with selective increases in directive, obstacle-oriented, and affective regulatory processes. Participatory-focus distributions, however, were broadly similar across conditions. These findings suggest that GenAI reshapes the distribution of regulatory responsibility in collaboration and offer implications for the human-centred design of AI-supported collaborative learning.

AI.tldr: GenAI availability in collaborative learning shifts regulation from socially shared to co-regulatory forms, redistributing regulatory responsibility while maintaining similar participatory focus patterns.
AI.motivation: Despite increasing use of Generative AI in collaborative learning, its effects on how groups regulate their collaboration processes (managing goals, participation, strategies, monitoring, and repair) remain unclear, particularly in terms of co-regulation and socially shared regulation dynamics.
AI.method: Parallel-group randomized experiment with 71 university students completing collaborative tasks either with GenAI available (Human-AI groups) or unavailable (Human-Human groups). Statistical analysis of human discourse focused on differences in collaborative regulation across regulatory modes, processes, and participatory focuses.
AI.result: GenAI availability shifted regulation away from predominantly socially shared forms towards more hybrid co-regulatory forms, with selective increases in directive, obstacle-oriented, and affective regulatory processes. Participatory-focus distributions remained broadly similar across conditions.
AI.conclusion: GenAI reshapes the distribution of regulatory responsibility in collaboration, suggesting implications for human-centred design of AI-supported collaborative learning environments.

title: Rethinking Residual Errors in Compensation-based LLM Quantization
summary: Methods based on weight compensation, which iteratively apply quantization and weight compensation to minimize the output error, have recently demonstrated remarkable success in quantizing Large Language Models (LLMs). The representative work, GPTQ, introduces several key techniques that make such iterative methods practical for LLMs with billions of parameters. GPTAQ extends this approach by introducing an asymmetric calibration process that aligns the output of each quantized layer with its full-precision counterpart, incorporating a residual error into the weight compensation framework. In this work, we revisit the formulation of the residual error. We identify a sub-optimal calibration objective in existing methods: during the intra-layer calibration process, they align the quantized output with the output from compensated weights, rather than the true output from the original full-precision model. Therefore, we redefine the objective to precisely align the quantized model's output with the original output of the full-precision model at each step. We then reveal that the residual error originates not only from the output difference of the preceding layer but also from the discrepancy between the compensated and original weights within each layer, which we name the 'compensation-aware error'. By inheriting the neuron decomposition technique from GPTAQ, we can efficiently incorporate this compensation-aware error into the weight update process. Extensive experiments on various LLMs and quantization settings demonstrate that our proposed enhancements integrate seamlessly with both GPTQ and GPTAQ, significantly improving their quantization performance. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/list0830/ResComp.

AI.tldr: A method that improves weight compensation-based LLM quantization by redefining the calibration objective to align with original full-precision outputs and incorporating compensation-aware error, showing significant performance improvements over GPTQ and GPTAQ.
AI.motivation: Existing weight compensation methods like GPTQ and GPTAQ have sub-optimal calibration objectives - they align quantized outputs with compensated weight outputs rather than the true original full-precision model outputs, leading to residual errors that degrade quantization performance.
AI.method: 1) Redefine the calibration objective to precisely align quantized model output with original full-precision model output at each step. 2) Identify that residual error comes from both preceding layer output differences AND compensation-original weight discrepancies (called "compensation-aware error"). 3) Use GPTAQ's neuron decomposition technique to efficiently incorporate compensation-aware error into weight updates.
AI.result: The proposed enhancements integrate seamlessly with both GPTQ and GPTAQ frameworks and significantly improve quantization performance across various LLMs and quantization settings. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides improved weight compensation formulations for LLM quantization by addressing fundamental limitations in existing calibration objectives and error sources, resulting in better alignment with original model performance.

title: ASPECT:Analogical Semantic Policy Execution via Language Conditioned Transfer
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents often struggle to generalize knowledge to new tasks, even those structurally similar to ones they have mastered. Although recent approaches have attempted to mitigate this issue via zero-shot transfer, they are often constrained by predefined, discrete class systems, limiting their adaptability to novel or compositional task variations. We propose a significantly more generalized approach, replacing discrete latent variables with natural language conditioning via a text-conditioned Variational Autoencoder (VAE). Our core innovation utilizes a Large Language Model (LLM) as a dynamic \textit{semantic operator} at test time. Rather than relying on rigid rules, our agent queries the LLM to semantically remap the description of the current observation to align with the source task. This source-aligned caption conditions the VAE to generate an imagined state compatible with the agent's original training, enabling direct policy reuse. By harnessing the flexible reasoning capabilities of LLMs, our approach achieves zero-shot transfer across a broad spectrum of complex and truly novel analogous tasks, moving beyond the limitations of fixed category mappings. Code and videos are available \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ASPECT-85C3/}{here}.

AI.tldr: Using LLMs as semantic operators to enable zero-shot RL transfer by generating source-aligned captions from novel observations, allowing direct policy reuse without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: RL agents fail to generalize to new tasks even when structurally similar, and existing zero-shot transfer methods are limited by predefined discrete class systems that can't adapt to novel or compositional task variations.
