Compared with the original LLaDA-2.0-mini, our method improves TPF on GSM8K from 2.04 to 5.47 while preserving accuracy. On MBPP, it increases TPF from 2.71 to 5.86 while maintaining comparable performance. On two H200 GPUs, our model achieves an average of 1,338 TPS at batch size 1. Code is available at: https://github.com/czg1225/DMax

AI.tldr: DMax is a new efficient diffusion language model paradigm that enables aggressive parallel decoding while preserving quality through progressive self-refinement from mask embeddings to token embeddings.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of error accumulation in parallel decoding for diffusion language models (dLLMs). Conventional masked dLLMs suffer from error propagation when performing parallel decoding, limiting their efficiency. The authors aim to enable more aggressive decoding parallelism while maintaining generation quality.
AI.method: DMax introduces three key innovations: 1) Reformulating decoding as progressive self-refinement from mask embeddings to token embeddings (instead of binary mask-to-token transition), 2) On-Policy Uniform Training that unifies masked and uniform dLLMs to recover from both masked inputs and erroneous predictions, and 3) Soft Parallel Decoding representing intermediate states as interpolations between predicted token embeddings and mask embeddings for iterative self-revising.
AI.result: Significant performance improvements: TPF (Tokens Per Forward) on GSM8K increased from 2.04 to 5.47 while preserving accuracy; on MBPP increased from 2.71 to 5.86 with comparable performance. The model achieves an average of 1,338 TPS (Tokens Per Second) at batch size 1 on two H200 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: DMax presents an effective paradigm for efficient diffusion language models that successfully mitigates error accumulation in parallel decoding, enabling substantial speed improvements while maintaining generation quality across various benchmarks.

title: Dead Weights, Live Signals: Feedforward Graphs of Frozen Language Models
summary: We present a feedforward graph architecture in which heterogeneous frozen large language models serve as computational nodes, communicating through a shared continuous latent space via learned linear projections. Building on recent work demonstrating geometric compatibility between independently trained LLM latent spaces~\cite{armstrong2026thinking}, we extend this finding from static two-model steering to end-to-end trainable multi-node graphs, where projection matrices are optimized jointly via backpropagation through residual stream injection hooks. Three small frozen models (Llama-3.2-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, Gemma-2-2B) encode the input into a shared latent space whose aggregate signal is injected into two larger frozen models (Phi-3-mini, Mistral-7B), whose representations feed a lightweight cross-attention output node. With only 17.6M trainable parameters against approximately 12B frozen, the architecture achieves 87.3\% on ARC-Challenge, 82.8\% on OpenBookQA, and 67.2\% on MMLU, outperforming the best single constituent model by 11.4, 6.2, and 1.2 percentage points respectively, and outperforming parameter-matched learned classifiers on frozen single models by 9.1, 5.2, and 6.7 points. Gradient flow through multiple frozen model boundaries is empirically verified to be tractable, and the output node develops selective routing behavior across layer-2 nodes without explicit supervision.

AI.tldr: A feedforward graph architecture with frozen LLMs as computational nodes communicating through learned latent space projections, achieving significant performance gains over individual models with minimal trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: To leverage multiple heterogeneous frozen large language models collaboratively while maintaining computational efficiency, exploring whether geometric compatibility between LLM latent spaces can enable effective multi-model coordination through learned projections.
AI.method: Build a feedforward graph where small frozen LLMs (Llama-3.2-1B, Qwen2.5-1.5B, Gemma-2-2B) encode inputs into a shared latent space via learned linear projections; aggregate signals are injected into larger frozen models (Phi-3-mini, Mistral-7B); their representations feed a lightweight cross-attention output node with only 17.6M trainable parameters. Projection matrices are optimized jointly via backpropagation through residual stream injection hooks.
AI.result: Achieves 87.3% on ARC-Challenge, 82.8% on OpenBookQA, and 67.2% on MMLU, outperforming the best single constituent model by 11.4, 6.2, and 1.2 percentage points respectively. Also outperforms parameter-matched learned classifiers on frozen single models by 9.1, 5.2, and 6.7 points. Gradient flow through multiple frozen model boundaries is tractable, and the output node develops selective routing behavior across layer-2 nodes without explicit supervision.
AI.conclusion: Heterogeneous frozen LLMs can be effectively coordinated through learned latent space projections in graph architectures, achieving significant performance improvements with minimal additional trainable parameters, demonstrating tractable gradient flow across frozen model boundaries and emergent selective routing behaviors.

title: Leveraging Complementary Embeddings for Replay Selection in Continual Learning with Small Buffers
summary: Catastrophic forgetting remains a key challenge in Continual Learning (CL). In replay-based CL with severe memory constraints, performance critically depends on the sample selection strategy for the replay buffer. Most existing approaches construct memory buffers using embeddings learned under supervised objectives. However, class-agnostic, self-supervised representations often encode rich, class-relevant semantics that are overlooked. We propose a new method, Multiple Embedding Replay Selection, MERS, which replaces the buffer selection module with a graph-based approach that integrates both supervised and self-supervised embeddings. Empirical results show consistent improvements over SOTA selection strategies across a range of continual learning algorithms, with particularly strong gains in low-memory regimes. On CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet, MERS outperforms single-embedding baselines without adding model parameters or increasing replay volume, making it a practical, drop-in enhancement for replay-based continual learning.

AI.tldr: MERS improves replay-based continual learning by using graph-based selection with both supervised and self-supervised embeddings to better select samples for memory buffers, achieving SOTA performance especially in low-memory scenarios.
AI.motivation: Catastrophic forgetting remains a major problem in continual learning. Most existing replay-based approaches use only supervised embeddings for buffer selection, overlooking potentially valuable class-relevant semantics encoded in self-supervised representations, particularly under severe memory constraints where sample selection strategy is critical.
AI.method: Proposed Multiple Embedding Replay Selection (MERS) method that replaces traditional buffer selection with a graph-based approach integrating both supervised and self-supervised embeddings. This enables more effective sample selection without adding model parameters or increasing replay buffer size.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over state-of-the-art selection strategies across various continual learning algorithms, with particularly strong gains in low-memory regimes. Outperforms single-embedding baselines on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet without additional computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: MERS is a practical, drop-in enhancement for replay-based continual learning that leverages complementary information from both supervised and self-supervised embeddings through graph-based selection, effectively addressing catastrophic forgetting under memory constraints.

title: EgoEverything: A Benchmark for Human Behavior Inspired Long Context Egocentric Video Understanding in AR Environment
summary: Long context egocentric video understanding has recently attracted significant research attention, with augmented reality (AR) highlighted as one of its most important application domains. Nevertheless, the task remains highly challenging due to the need for reasoning over extended temporal contexts and diverse, unstructured activities. Although several benchmarks exist, most egocentric datasets rely on human worn cameras and focus mainly on visual content, with limited consideration of underlying user behavior when forming video-related queries. EgoEverything is a benchmark that explicitly considers human behavior by leveraging human attention signals, abstracted from gaze data, when generating questions. It comprises over 5,000 multiple choice question answer pairs, spanning more than 100 hours of video. By integrating human attention signals during question generation, it more faithfully captures natural human behavior and offers a realistic evaluation setting for long-context egocentric video understanding in AR.

AI.tldr: EgoEverything is a new benchmark for long-context egocentric video understanding that incorporates human attention signals from gaze data to generate more realistic questions, featuring over 5,000 MC questions across 100+ hours of video.
AI.motivation: Existing egocentric video benchmarks mainly use human-worn cameras and focus on visual content while neglecting user behavior when forming queries, creating unrealistic evaluation settings that don't capture natural human behavior patterns.
AI.method: The authors created EgoEverything by leveraging human attention signals abstracted from gaze data to generate questions, ensuring the benchmark more faithfully reflects natural human behavior patterns in query formation for egocentric video understanding.
AI.result: EgoEverything benchmark contains over 5,000 multiple-choice question-answer pairs spanning more than 100 hours of video, explicitly integrating human attention signals to provide a more realistic evaluation setting for AR applications.
AI.conclusion: By incorporating human attention signals during question generation, EgoEverything offers a more faithful representation of natural human behavior and provides a better evaluation framework for long-context egocentric video understanding in augmented reality applications.

title: Bias-Constrained Diffusion Schedules for PDE Emulations: Reconstruction Error Minimization and Efficient Unrolled Training
summary: Conditional Diffusion Models are powerful surrogates for emulating complex spatiotemporal dynamics, yet they often fail to match the accuracy of deterministic neural emulators for high-precision tasks. In this work, we address two critical limitations of autoregressive PDE diffusion models: their sub-optimal single-step accuracy and the prohibitive computational cost of unrolled training. First, we characterize the relationship between the noise schedule, the reconstruction error reduction rate and the diffusion exposure bias, demonstrating that standard schedules lead to suboptimal reconstruction error. Leveraging this insight, we propose an \textit{Adaptive Noise Schedule} framework that minimizes inference reconstruction error by dynamically constraining the model's exposure bias. We further show that this optimized schedule enables a fast \textit{Proxy Unrolled Training} method to stabilize long-term rollouts without the cost of full Markov Chain sampling. Both proposed methods enable significant improvements in short-term accuracy and long-term stability over diffusion and deterministic baselines on diverse benchmarks, including forced Navier-Stokes, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky and Transonic Flow.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses limitations of autoregressive PDE diffusion models by proposing an Adaptive Noise Schedule framework to improve single-step accuracy and a Proxy Unrolled Training method to reduce computational costs while enhancing long-term stability.
AI.motivation: Conditional diffusion models, while powerful for emulating spatiotemporal dynamics, often fall short of deterministic neural emulators in high-precision tasks. The authors identify two key limitations: sub-optimal single-step accuracy and prohibitive computational costs of unrolled training in autoregressive PDE diffusion models.
AI.method: 1) Characterized the relationship between noise schedule, reconstruction error reduction rate, and diffusion exposure bias, showing standard schedules lead to suboptimal reconstruction error. 2) Proposed an Adaptive Noise Schedule framework that minimizes inference reconstruction error by dynamically constraining exposure bias. 3) Developed a fast Proxy Unrolled Training method that leverages the optimized schedule to stabilize long-term rollouts without the computational cost of full Markov Chain sampling.
AI.result: The proposed methods achieve significant improvements in both short-term accuracy and long-term stability over diffusion and deterministic baselines across diverse benchmarks including forced Navier-Stokes, Kuramoto-Sivashinsky, and Transonic Flow equations.
AI.conclusion: The Adaptive Noise Schedule and Proxy Unrolled Training methods address fundamental limitations of current diffusion models for PDE emulation, providing both improved accuracy and computational efficiency while maintaining the benefits of probabilistic modeling for complex spatiotemporal dynamics.

title: Scaling-Aware Data Selection for End-to-End Autonomous Driving Systems
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026, 8 pages of main body and 10 pages of appendix
summary: Large-scale deep learning models for physical AI applications depend on diverse training data collection efforts. These models and correspondingly, the training data, must address different evaluation criteria necessary for the models to be deployable in real-world environments. Data selection policies can guide the development of the training set, but current frameworks do not account for the ambiguity in how data points affect different metrics. In this work, we propose Mixture Optimization via Scaling-Aware Iterative Collection (MOSAIC), a general data selection framework that operates by: (i) partitioning the dataset into domains; (ii) fitting neural scaling laws from each data domain to the evaluation metrics; and (iii) optimizing a data mixture by iteratively adding data from domains that maximize the change in metrics. We apply MOSAIC to autonomous driving (AD), where an End-to-End (E2E) planner model is evaluated on the Extended Predictive Driver Model Score (EPDMS), an aggregate of driving rule compliance metrics. Here, MOSAIC outperforms a diverse set of baselines on EPDMS with up to 80\% less data.

AI.tldr: MOSAIC is a data selection framework that partitions datasets into domains, fits neural scaling laws, and iteratively adds data from domains that maximize metric improvement, achieving 80% data reduction for autonomous driving planner models.
AI.motivation: Current data selection frameworks for large-scale deep learning models in physical AI applications don't account for ambiguity in how data points affect different evaluation metrics, which is crucial for real-world deployment.
AI.method: Mixture Optimization via Scaling-Aware Iterative Collection (MOSAIC) framework: (1) partitions dataset into domains, (2) fits neural scaling laws from each domain to evaluation metrics, (3) optimizes data mixture by iteratively adding data from domains that maximize metric change.
AI.result: MOSAIC outperforms diverse baselines on Extended Predictive Driver Model Score (EPDMS) for autonomous driving End-to-End planner models with up to 80% less data required.
AI.conclusion: MOSAIC provides an effective general framework for data selection that accounts for metric ambiguity and enables more efficient training data collection for physical AI applications, particularly valuable for autonomous driving where diverse evaluation metrics must be balanced.

title: SOLAR: Communication-Efficient Model Adaptation via Subspace-Oriented Latent Adapter Reparametrization
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, such as LoRA, enable scalable adaptation of foundation models by injecting low-rank adapters. However, their communication and storage costs remain a major bottleneck in resource-constrained settings. We propose SOLAR (Subspace-Oriented Latent Adapter Reparameterization), a post-training compression framework that substantially reduces the communication cost (i.e., the number of parameters to transmit or store) of PEFT adapters. SOLAR expresses each PEFT update as a linear combination of basis vectors formed from the foundation model's singular vectors with controlled random perturbations. By exploiting the subspace similarity (the alignment of principal directions) between the foundation model and task-specific fine-tuned updates, SOLAR decouples the adapter size from PEFT structure and ensures compact yet expressive representations. It is model-agnostic and compatible with existing PEFT methods, including LoRA, AdaLoRA, and other adapter modules. We theoretically establish a bound on the reconstruction error. Experiments on language and vision tasks using LLaMA, GPT, and ViT models demonstrate that SOLAR preserves task performance while significantly reducing model representation sizes, offering an effective and communication-efficient solution for deployment in distributed systems and edge devices.

AI.tldr: SOLAR is a post-training compression framework that significantly reduces communication and storage costs for PEFT adapters by expressing them as linear combinations of basis vectors from the foundation model's singular vectors with perturbations.
AI.motivation: PEFT methods like LoRA enable scalable adaptation of foundation models, but their communication and storage costs remain a major bottleneck in resource-constrained settings like distributed systems and edge devices.
AI.method: SOLAR expresses each PEFT update as a linear combination of basis vectors formed from the foundation model's singular vectors with controlled random perturbations. It exploits subspace similarity between foundation models and task-specific updates, decoupling adapter size from PEFT structure.
AI.result: SOLAR preserves task performance while significantly reducing model representation sizes. Experiments on language and vision tasks with LLaMA, GPT, and ViT models demonstrate its effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: SOLAR offers an effective, communication-efficient solution for deploying PEFT adapters in resource-constrained environments, is model-agnostic and compatible with existing PEFT methods, and has theoretical bounds on reconstruction error.

title: ADAPTive Input Training for Many-to-One Pre-Training on Time-Series Classification
summary: Recent work on time-series models has leveraged self-supervised training to learn meaningful features and patterns in order to improve performance on downstream tasks and generalize to unseen modalities. While these pretraining methods have shown great promise in one-to-many scenarios, where a model is pre-trained on one dataset and fine-tuned on a downstream dataset, they have struggled to generalize to new datasets when more datasets are added during pre-training. This is a fundamental challenge in building foundation models for time-series data, as it limits the ability to develop models that can learn from a large variety of diverse datasets available. To address this challenge, we present a new pre-training paradigm for time-series data called ADAPT, which can efficiently align the physical properties of data in the time-series domain, enabling mixed-batch pre-training despite the extreme discrepancies in the input sizes and channel dimensions of pre-training data. We trained on 162 time-series classification datasets and set new state-of-the-art performance for classification benchmarks. We successfully train a model within the time-series domain on a wide range of datasets simultaneously, which is a major building block for building generalist foundation models in time-series domains.

AI.tldr: ADAPT is a new pre-training paradigm for time-series data that enables mixed-batch training across diverse datasets with different input sizes and channel dimensions, achieving state-of-the-art performance on classification benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing time-series pre-training methods struggle to generalize when multiple diverse datasets are added during pre-training, limiting the development of foundation models that can learn from the wide variety of available time-series data.
AI.method: ADAPT efficiently aligns the physical properties of time-series data despite extreme discrepancies in input sizes and channel dimensions, enabling mixed-batch pre-training across 162 diverse time-series classification datasets.
AI.result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on classification benchmarks by successfully training a model on a wide range of datasets simultaneously, establishing a major building block for generalist foundation models in time-series domains.
AI.conclusion: ADAPT addresses the fundamental challenge of multi-dataset pre-training for time-series foundation models by enabling efficient alignment of physical properties across diverse datasets, paving the way for more robust and generalizable time-series models.

title: Zero-shot Multivariate Time Series Forecasting Using Tabular Prior Fitted Networks
summary: Tabular foundation models, particularly Prior-data Fitted Networks like TabPFN have emerged as the leading contender in a myriad of tasks ranging from data imputation to label prediction on the tabular data format surpassing the historical successes of tree-based models. This has led to investigations on their applicability to forecasting time series data which can be formulated as a tabular problem. While recent work to this end has displayed positive results, most works have limited their treatment of multivariate time series problems to several independent univariate time series forecasting subproblems, thus ignoring any inter-channel interactions. Overcoming this limitation, we introduce a generally applicable framework for multivariate time series forecasting using tabular foundation models. We achieve this by recasting the multivariate time series forecasting problem as a series of scalar regression problems which can then be solved zero-shot by any tabular foundation model with regression capabilities. We present results of our method using the TabPFN-TS backbone and compare performance with the current state of the art tabular methods.

AI.tldr: A framework that adapts tabular foundation models (like TabPFN) for multivariate time series forecasting by reformulating the problem as a series of scalar regression problems that can be solved zero-shot, addressing previous limitations in handling inter-channel interactions.
AI.motivation: While tabular foundation models have shown success in various tasks and some attempts have been made to apply them to time series forecasting, existing approaches typically treat multivariate time series as independent univariate subproblems, ignoring important inter-channel interactions that could improve forecasting accuracy.
AI.method: The authors introduce a framework that recasts multivariate time series forecasting as a series of scalar regression problems. This reformulation allows any tabular foundation model with regression capabilities (specifically using TabPFN-TS as the backbone) to solve these problems in a zero-shot manner, preserving inter-channel dependencies.
AI.result: The method shows positive results when evaluated with the TabPFN-TS backbone, outperforming current state-of-the-art tabular methods for multivariate time series forecasting. The framework successfully handles inter-channel interactions that previous approaches ignored.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully extends tabular foundation models to multivariate time series forecasting while addressing the critical limitation of inter-channel interaction handling, establishing a new direction for leveraging tabular models in time series analysis with promising performance improvements.

title: Adversarial Label Invariant Graph Data Augmentations for Out-of-Distribution Generalization
comment: 21 pages, 3 figures, accepted at ICML SCIS 2023
summary: Out-of-distribution (OoD) generalization occurs when representation learning encounters a distribution shift. This occurs frequently in practice when training and testing data come from different environments. Covariate shift is a type of distribution shift that occurs only in the input data, while the concept distribution stays invariant. We propose RIA - Regularization for Invariance with Adversarial training, a new method for OoD generalization under convariate shift. Motivated by an analogy to $Q$-learning, it performs an adversarial exploration for training data environments. These new environments are induced by adversarial label invariant data augmentations that prevent a collapse to an in-distribution trained learner. It works with many existing OoD generalization methods for covariate shift that can be formulated as constrained optimization problems. We develop an alternating gradient descent-ascent algorithm to solve the problem, and perform extensive experiments on OoD graph classification for various kinds of synthetic and natural distribution shifts. We demonstrate that our method can achieve high accuracy compared with OoD baselines.

AI.tldr: RIA (Regularization for Invariance with Adversarial training) is a method for out-of-distribution generalization under covariate shift that uses adversarial exploration of training data environments through label-invariant data augmentations.
AI.motivation: Out-of-distribution generalization is crucial when training and testing data come from different environments. Covariate shift (input distribution changes while concept stays the same) frequently occurs in practice, and existing methods need improvement for handling such distribution shifts.
AI.method: RIA performs adversarial exploration for training data environments using adversarial label-invariant data augmentations. It formulates OoD generalization as constrained optimization problems and develops an alternating gradient descent-ascent algorithm to solve them, preventing collapse to in-distribution trained learners.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on OoD graph classification with various synthetic and natural distribution shifts demonstrate that RIA achieves high accuracy compared with existing OoD baselines.
AI.conclusion: RIA provides an effective adversarial training approach for covariate shift problems that can work with many existing OoD generalization methods and shows strong empirical performance on graph classification tasks with distribution shifts.

title: KV Cache Offloading for Context-Intensive Tasks
summary: With the growing demand for long-context LLMs across a wide range of applications, the key-value (KV) cache has become a critical bottleneck for both latency and memory usage. Recently, KV-cache offloading has emerged as a promising approach to reduce memory footprint and inference latency while preserving accuracy. Prior evaluations have largely focused on tasks that do not require extracting large amounts of information from the context. In this work, we study KV-cache offloading on context-intensive tasks: problems where the solution requires looking up a lot of information from the input prompt. We create and release the Text2JSON benchmark, a highly context-intensive task that requires extracting structured knowledge from raw text. We evaluate modern KV offloading on Text2JSON and other context-intensive tasks and find significant performance degradation on both Llama 3 and Qwen 3 models. Our analysis identifies two key reasons for poor accuracy: low-rank projection of keys and unreliable landmarks, and proposes a simpler alternative strategy that significantly improves accuracy across multiple LLM families and benchmarks. These findings highlight the need for a comprehensive and rigorous evaluation of long-context compression techniques.

AI.tldr: KV-cache offloading degrades significantly on context-intensive tasks, new Text2JSON benchmark reveals limitations, proposed simpler alternative improves accuracy
AI.motivation: KV cache is a critical bottleneck for long-context LLMs in latency and memory usage. While KV-cache offloading has emerged as promising, prior evaluations focused on tasks that don't require extracting large amounts of information from context. There's a need to evaluate how these techniques perform on context-intensive tasks.
AI.method: Created and released the Text2JSON benchmark, a highly context-intensive task requiring structured knowledge extraction from raw text. Evaluated modern KV offloading on Text2JSON and other context-intensive tasks using Llama 3 and Qwen 3 models. Analyzed performance degradation reasons and proposed an alternative strategy.
AI.result: Found significant performance degradation on context-intensive tasks with modern KV offloading. Identified two key reasons: low-rank projection of keys and unreliable landmarks. Proposed a simpler alternative strategy that significantly improves accuracy across multiple LLM families and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Current KV-cache offloading techniques have major limitations on context-intensive tasks. The findings highlight the need for comprehensive and rigorous evaluation of long-context compression techniques beyond standard benchmarks.

title: Provably Adaptive Linear Approximation for the Shapley Value and Beyond
summary: The Shapley value, and its broader family of semi-values, has received much attention in various attribution problems. A fundamental and long-standing challenge is their efficient approximation, since exact computation generally requires an exponential number of utility queries in the number of players $n$. To meet the challenges of large-scale applications, we explore the limits of efficiently approximating semi-values under a $Θ(n)$ space constraint. Building upon a vector concentration inequality, we establish a theoretical framework that enables sharper query complexities for existing unbiased randomized algorithms. Within this framework, we systematically develop a linear-space algorithm that requires $O(\frac{n}{ε^{2}}\log\frac{1}δ)$ utility queries to ensure $P(\|\hat{\boldsymbolφ}-\boldsymbolφ\|_{2}\geqε)\leq δ$ for all commonly used semi-values. In particular, our framework naturally bridges OFA, unbiased kernelSHAP, SHAP-IQ and the regression-adjusted approach, and definitively characterizes when paired sampling is beneficial. Moreover, our algorithm allows explicit minimization of the mean square error for each specific utility function. Accordingly, we introduce the first adaptive, linear-time, linear-space randomized algorithm, Adalina, that theoretically achieves improved mean square error. All of our theoretical findings are experimentally validated.

AI.tldr: The paper develops efficient approximation algorithms for Shapley values and semi-values under linear space constraints, achieving improved query complexities and introducing an adaptive algorithm called Adalina.
AI.motivation: The fundamental challenge of efficiently approximating Shapley values and semi-values, which require exponential computation in exact form for n players. There's a need for scalable algorithms that work within practical space constraints (Θ(n)) for large-scale applications.
AI.method: The authors establish a theoretical framework using vector concentration inequalities to enable sharper query complexity bounds. They develop linear-space algorithms requiring O(n/ε² log 1/δ) utility queries, unify existing methods (OFA, unbiased kernelSHAP, SHAP-IQ, regression-adjusted approach), and introduce Adalina - an adaptive, linear-time, linear-space randomized algorithm that minimizes mean square error for specific utility functions.
AI.result: The paper presents: 1) Theoretical framework enabling improved query complexities, 2) Linear-space algorithm with O(n/ε² log 1/δ) query complexity for common semi-values, 3) Unified understanding of existing methods, 4) Characterization of when paired sampling is beneficial, 5) Adalina algorithm achieving improved mean square error theoretically, 6) Experimental validation of all theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: The work provides significant advances in efficient approximation of semi-values under space constraints, bridging theoretical understanding with practical algorithms that scale to large problems while maintaining strong probabilistic guarantees.

title: Less Approximates More: Harmonizing Performance and Confidence Faithfulness via Hybrid Post-Training for High-Stakes Tasks
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes tasks, where confident yet incorrect inferences may cause severe real-world harm, bringing the previously overlooked issue of confidence faithfulness back to the forefront. A promising solution is to jointly optimize unsupervised Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) with reasoning-trace-guided Reasoning Distillation (RD), which may face three persistent challenges: scarcity of high-quality training corpora, factually unwarranted overconfidence and indiscriminate fusion that amplifies erroneous updates. Inspired by the human confidence accumulation from uncertainty to certainty, we propose Progressive Reasoning Gain (PRG) to measure whether reasoning steps progressively strengthen support for the final answer. Furthermore, we introduce HyTuning, a hybrid post-training framework that adaptively reweights RD and RLIF via a PRG-style metric, using scarce supervised reasoning traces as a stable anchor while exploiting abundant unlabeled queries for scalability. Experiments on several domain-specific and general benchmarks demonstrate that HyTuning improves accuracy while achieving confidence faithfulness under limited supervision, supporting a practical "Less Approximates More" effect.

AI.tldr: HyTuning is a hybrid post-training framework that improves LLM confidence faithfulness by adaptively reweighting reasoning distillation and reinforcement learning from internal feedback using a progressive reasoning gain metric.
AI.motivation: LLMs in high-stakes tasks require confidence faithfulness, but current methods face challenges: scarcity of high-quality training corpora, factually unwarranted overconfidence, and indiscriminate fusion that amplifies erroneous updates.
AI.method: Introduces Progressive Reasoning Gain (PRG) to measure if reasoning steps progressively strengthen support for the final answer. HyTuning adaptively reweights Reasoning Distillation (RD) and Reinforcement Learning from Internal Feedback (RLIF) via PRG-style metrics, using scarce supervised reasoning traces as anchor while exploiting abundant unlabeled queries.
AI.result: Experiments on domain-specific and general benchmarks show HyTuning improves accuracy while achieving confidence faithfulness under limited supervision, demonstrating a "Less Approximates More" effect.
AI.conclusion: HyTuning effectively addresses confidence faithfulness in LLMs through progressive reasoning measurement and hybrid optimization, offering practical benefits for deployment in high-stakes applications with limited supervision.

title: A Machine Learning Framework for Turbofan Health Estimation via Inverse Problem Formulation
summary: Estimating the health state of turbofan engines is a challenging ill-posed inverse problem, hindered by sparse sensing and complex nonlinear thermodynamics. Research in this area remains fragmented, with comparisons limited by the use of unrealistic datasets and insufficient exploration of the exploitation of temporal information. This work investigates how to recover component-level health indicators from operational sensor data under realistic degradation and maintenance patterns. To support this study, we introduce a new dataset that incorporates industry-oriented complexities such as maintenance events and usage changes. Using this dataset, we establish an initial benchmark that compares steady-state and nonstationary data-driven models, and Bayesian filters, classic families of methods used to solve this problem. In addition to this benchmark, we introduce self-supervised learning (SSL) approaches that learn latent representations without access to true health labels, a scenario reflective of real-world operational constraints. By comparing the downstream estimation performance of these unsupervised representations against the direct prediction baselines, we establish a practical lower bound on the difficulty of solving this inverse problem. Our results reveal that traditional filters remain strong baselines, while SSL methods reveal the intrinsic complexity of health estimation and highlight the need for more advanced and interpretable inference strategies. For reproducibility, both the generated dataset and the implementation used in this work are made accessible.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a new realistic dataset for turbofan engine health estimation, establishes a benchmark comparing different methods, explores self-supervised learning approaches, and finds that traditional Bayesian filters remain strong baselines while highlighting the intrinsic complexity of the problem.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the fragmented research in turbofan engine health estimation, which suffers from unrealistic datasets and insufficient exploration of temporal information exploitation. The paper aims to recover component-level health indicators under realistic degradation and maintenance patterns.
AI.method: The authors introduce a new industry-oriented dataset with maintenance events and usage changes. They establish a benchmark comparing steady-state vs. nonstationary data-driven models and Bayesian filters. Additionally, they introduce self-supervised learning (SSL) approaches that learn latent representations without true health labels.
AI.result: Results show that traditional Bayesian filters remain strong baselines for health estimation. SSL methods help establish a practical lower bound on the difficulty of solving this inverse problem, revealing the intrinsic complexity of health estimation and highlighting the need for more advanced and interpretable inference strategies.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the value of realistic datasets and proper benchmarking in turbofan health estimation. While traditional methods perform well, the complexity revealed by SSL approaches suggests the need for more sophisticated inference methods. The dataset and implementation are made available for reproducibility.

title: TTVS: Boosting Self-Exploring Reinforcement Learning via Test-time Variational Synthesis
summary: Despite significant advances in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) driven by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), this paradigm is fundamentally limited in specialized or novel domains where such supervision is prohibitively expensive or unavailable, posing a key challenge for test-time adaptation. While existing test-time methods offer a potential solution, they are constrained by learning from static query sets, risking overfitting to textual patterns. To address this gap, we introduce Test-Time Variational Synthesis (TTVS), a novel framework that enables LRMs to self-evolve by dynamically augmenting the training stream from unlabeled test queries. TTVS comprises two synergistic modules: (1) Online Variational Synthesis, which transforms static test queries into a dynamic stream of diverse, semantically-equivalent variations, enforcing the model to learn underlying problem logic rather than superficial patterns; (2) Test-time Hybrid Exploration, which balances accuracy-driven exploitation with consistency-driven exploration across synthetic variants. Extensive experiments show TTVS yields superior performance across eight model architectures. Notably, using only unlabeled test-time data, TTVS not only surpasses other test-time adaptation methods but also outperforms state-of-the-art supervised RL-based techniques trained on vast, high-quality labeled data.

AI.tldr: TTVS enables Large Reasoning Models to self-evolve during test time by dynamically generating diverse variations of unlabeled queries and balancing accuracy-driven exploitation with consistency-driven exploration, achieving superior performance without expensive supervision.
AI.motivation: Current reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for Large Reasoning Models requires expensive supervision that's unavailable in specialized or novel domains, and existing test-time methods risk overfitting to static query patterns rather than learning underlying problem logic.
AI.method: TTVS (Test-Time Variational Synthesis) introduces two modules: 1) Online Variational Synthesis that transforms static test queries into dynamic, semantically-equivalent variations to force learning of problem logic; 2) Test-time Hybrid Exploration that balances accuracy-driven exploitation with consistency-driven exploration across synthetic variants.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show TTVS yields superior performance across eight model architectures. Using only unlabeled test-time data, TTVS surpasses other test-time adaptation methods and outperforms state-of-the-art supervised RL-based techniques trained on large labeled datasets.
AI.conclusion: TTVS provides an effective framework for test-time adaptation of Large Reasoning Models in domains where expensive supervision is unavailable, enabling models to self-evolve by learning from dynamically generated variations of unlabeled test queries.

title: Persistence-Augmented Neural Networks
summary: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) provides tools to describe the shape of data, but integrating topological features into deep learning pipelines remains challenging, especially when preserving local geometric structure rather than summarizing it globally. We propose a persistence-based data augmentation framework that encodes local gradient flow regions and their hierarchical evolution using the Morse-Smale complex. This representation, compatible with both convolutional and graph neural networks, retains spatially localized topological information across multiple scales. Importantly, the augmentation procedure itself is efficient, with computational complexity $O(n \log n)$, making it practical for large datasets. We evaluate our method on histopathology image classification and 3D porous material regression, where it consistently outperforms baselines and global TDA descriptors such as persistence images and landscapes. We also show that pruning the base level of the hierarchy reduces memory usage while maintaining competitive performance. These results highlight the potential of local, structured topological augmentation for scalable and interpretable learning across data modalities.

AI.tldr: A new persistence-based data augmentation method using Morse-Smale complexes to encode local topological features efficiently, improving performance in applications like histopathology and 3D material analysis.
AI.motivation: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) tools can describe data shape, but integrating topological features into deep learning is challenging, especially when preserving local geometric structure rather than just global summaries.
AI.method: Propose a persistence-based data augmentation framework that encodes local gradient flow regions and their hierarchical evolution using the Morse-Smale complex. This representation works with both convolutional and graph neural networks and retains spatially localized topological information across scales with O(n log n) computational complexity.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines and global TDA descriptors (persistence images and landscapes) on histopathology image classification and 3D porous material regression. Pruning the base level of hierarchy reduces memory usage while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.conclusion: The method demonstrates potential for local, structured topological augmentation to enable scalable and interpretable learning across different data modalities.

title: Quantization Impact on the Accuracy and Communication Efficiency Trade-off in Federated Learning for Aerospace Predictive Maintenance
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving predictive maintenance across distributed aerospace fleets, but gradient communication overhead constrains deployment on bandwidth-limited IoT nodes. This paper investigates the impact of symmetric uniform quantization ($b \in \{32,8,4,2\}$ bits) on the accuracy--efficiency trade-off of a custom-designed lightweight 1-D convolutional model (AeroConv1D, 9\,697 parameters) trained via FL on the NASA C-MAPSS benchmark under a realistic Non-IID client partition. Using a rigorous multi-seed evaluation ($N=10$ seeds), we show that INT4 achieves accuracy \emph{statistically indistinguishable} from FP32 on both FD001 ($p=0.341$) and FD002 ($p=0.264$ MAE, $p=0.534$ NASA score) while delivering an $8\times$ reduction in gradient communication cost (37.88~KiB $\to$ 4.73~KiB per round). A key methodological finding is that naïve IID client partitioning artificially suppresses variance; correct Non-IID evaluation reveals the true operational instability of extreme quantization, demonstrated via a direct empirical IID vs.\ Non-IID comparison. INT2 is empirically characterized as unsuitable: while it achieves lower MAE on FD002 through extreme quantization-induced over-regularization, this apparent gain is accompanied by catastrophic NASA score instability (CV\,=\,45.8\% vs.\ 22.3\% for FP32), confirming non-reproducibility under heterogeneous operating conditions. Analytical FPGA resource projections on the Xilinx ZCU102 confirm that INT4 fits within hardware constraints (85.5\% DSP utilization), potentially enabling a complete FL pipeline on a single SoC. The full simulation codebase and FPGA estimation scripts are publicly available at https://github.com/therealdeadbeef/aerospace-fl-quantization.

AI.tldr: Quantizing federated learning from FP32 to INT4 preserves predictive maintenance accuracy while cutting gradient communication 8×, enabling deployment on bandwidth-limited aerospace IoT nodes.
AI.motivation: Federated learning enables privacy-preserving predictive maintenance in aerospace but faces gradient communication bottlenecks on bandwidth-limited IoT nodes.
AI.method: Used symmetric uniform quantization (32, 8, 4, 2 bits) on a lightweight 1-D convolutional model trained via FL on NASA C-MAPSS benchmark with rigorous multi-seed evaluation (N=10) and realistic Non-IID client partitioning.
AI.result: INT4 achieves statistically indistinguishable accuracy from FP32 while delivering 8× gradient communication reduction; INT2 shows unstable performance; FPGA analysis confirms INT4 fits hardware constraints.
AI.conclusion: INT4 quantization is optimal for aerospace FL deployment, balancing accuracy preservation with significant communication savings, while proper Non-IID evaluation is crucial for assessing real-world operational stability.

title: The Impact of Dimensionality on the Stability of Node Embeddings
summary: Previous work has established that neural network-based node embeddings return different outcomes when trained with identical parameters on the same dataset, just from using different training seeds. Yet, it has not been thoroughly analyzed how key hyperparameters such as embedding dimension could impact this instability. In this work, we investigate how varying the dimensionality of node embeddings influences both their stability and downstream performance. We systematically evaluate five widely used methods -- ASNE, DGI, GraphSAGE, node2vec, and VERSE -- across multiple datasets and embedding dimensions. We assess stability from both a representational perspective and a functional perspective, alongside performance evaluation. Our results show that embedding stability varies significantly with dimensionality, but we observe different patterns across the methods we consider: while some approaches, such as node2vec and ASNE, tend to become more stable with higher dimensionality, other methods do not exhibit the same trend. Moreover, we find that maximum stability does not necessarily align with optimal task performance. These findings highlight the importance of carefully selecting embedding dimension, and provide new insights into the trade-offs between stability, performance, and computational effectiveness in graph representation learning.

AI.tldr: Higher embedding dimensions don't always increase stability in graph neural networks, and maximum stability doesn't always match optimal task performance - careful dimension selection is crucial.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed neural network-based node embeddings return different outcomes with same parameters but different training seeds (instability), but it hasn't been thoroughly analyzed how key hyperparameters like embedding dimension impact this instability.
AI.method: Systematically evaluated five widely used methods (ASNE, DGI, GraphSAGE, node2vec, VERSE) across multiple datasets and embedding dimensions. Assessed stability from both representational and functional perspectives alongside performance evaluation.
AI.result: Embedding stability varies significantly with dimensionality, but different patterns across methods: some approaches (node2vec, ASNE) become more stable with higher dimensionality, others don't show same trend. Maximum stability doesn't necessarily align with optimal task performance.
AI.conclusion: Highlights importance of carefully selecting embedding dimension, provides new insights into trade-offs between stability, performance, and computational effectiveness in graph representation learning.

title: What Drives Representation Steering? A Mechanistic Case Study on Steering Refusal
summary: Applying steering vectors to large language models (LLMs) is an efficient and effective model alignment technique, but we lack an interpretable explanation for how it works-- specifically, what internal mechanisms steering vectors affect and how this results in different model outputs. To investigate the causal mechanisms underlying the effectiveness of steering vectors, we conduct a comprehensive case study on refusal. We propose a multi-token activation patching framework and discover that different steering methodologies leverage functionally interchangeable circuits when applied at the same layer. These circuits reveal that steering vectors primarily interact with the attention mechanism through the OV circuit while largely ignoring the QK circuit-- freezing all attention scores during steering drops performance by only 8.75% across two model families. A mathematical decomposition of the steered OV circuit further reveals semantically interpretable concepts, even in cases where the steering vector itself does not. Leveraging the activation patching results, we show that steering vectors can be sparsified by up to 90-99% while retaining most performance, and that different steering methodologies agree on a subset of important dimensions.

AI.tldr: Steering vectors affect LLMs primarily through OV circuits, not QK circuits, and can be heavily sparsified while retaining effectiveness.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of interpretable explanation for how steering vectors work in LLM alignment - specifically what internal mechanisms they affect and how this leads to different outputs.
AI.method: Comprehensive case study on refusal behavior using multi-token activation patching framework to analyze causal mechanisms, mathematical decomposition of steered OV circuits, and sparsity experiments.
AI.result: Discovered steering vectors primarily interact through OV circuits (not QK circuits), freezing attention scores only drops performance by 8.75%. Steering vectors can be sparsified 90-99% while keeping most performance, and different methods agree on important dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Steering vectors work through functionally interchangeable circuits that affect OV mechanisms, enabling interpretable concept discovery and practical sparsification while maintaining effectiveness.

title: Meta-learning In-Context Enables Training-Free Cross Subject Brain Decoding
summary: Visual decoding from brain signals is a key challenge at the intersection of computer vision and neuroscience, requiring methods that bridge neural representations and computational models of vision. A field-wide goal is to achieve generalizable, cross-subject models. A major obstacle towards this goal is the substantial variability in neural representations across individuals, which has so far required training bespoke models or fine-tuning separately for each subject. To address this challenge, we introduce a meta-optimized approach for semantic visual decoding from fMRI that generalizes to novel subjects without any fine-tuning. By simply conditioning on a small set of image-brain activation examples from the new individual, our model rapidly infers their unique neural encoding patterns to facilitate robust and efficient visual decoding. Our approach is explicitly optimized for in-context learning of the new subject's encoding model and performs decoding by hierarchical inference, inverting the encoder. First, for multiple brain regions, we estimate the per-voxel visual response encoder parameters by constructing a context over multiple stimuli and responses. Second, we construct a context consisting of encoder parameters and response values over multiple voxels to perform aggregated functional inversion. We demonstrate strong cross-subject and cross-scanner generalization across diverse visual backbones without retraining or fine-tuning. Moreover, our approach requires neither anatomical alignment nor stimulus overlap. This work is a critical step towards a generalizable foundation model for non-invasive brain decoding.

AI.tldr: A meta-optimized approach for cross-subject visual decoding from fMRI that generalizes to new subjects without fine-tuning by using in-context learning of individual neural encoding patterns.
AI.motivation: Neural representations vary substantially across individuals, requiring bespoke models or fine-tuning for each subject, which hinders development of generalizable, cross-subject brain decoding models.
AI.method: A meta-optimized approach that conditions on a small set of image-brain activation examples from a new subject, performs hierarchical inference: 1) estimates per-voxel visual response encoder parameters using stimulus-response contexts, 2) aggregates functional inversion using contexts of encoder parameters and response values across voxels.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong cross-subject and cross-scanner generalization across diverse visual backbones without retraining or fine-tuning, requiring neither anatomical alignment nor stimulus overlap.
AI.conclusion: The approach represents a critical step toward a generalizable foundation model for non-invasive brain decoding by enabling zero-shot adaptation to new individuals.
